channel.title: Compile-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Compile-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] C++ clang-tidy - lint error
item.description:
clang-tidy - lint error

clang-tidy is a Clang-based linter that finds common programming errors and suggests modernization improvements.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Modernize use auto
std::vector<int>::iterator it = vec.begin(); // clang-tidy suggests auto

// Cause 2: Use nullptr instead of NULL
int* p = NULL; // clang-tidy: use nullptr

// Cause 3: Use range-based for
for (int i = 0; i < vec.size(); i++) {
 process(vec[i]); // clang-tidy: use range-based for
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Run clang-tidy

clang-tidy src/*.cpp -- -std=c++17

Fix 2: Apply fixes automatically

clang-tidy src/*.cpp --fix -- -std=c++17

Fix 3: Fix specific checks

// modernize-use-auto
auto it = vec.begin(); // use auto

// modernize-use-nullptr
int* p = nullptr; // use nullptr

// modernize-loop-convert
for (auto& item : vec) {
 process(item);
}

Related Errors

  • cppcheck - static analysis — cppcheck errors.
  • AddressSanitizer — memory errors.
  • Valgrind - memory error — memory errors.

item.title: [Solution] C++ CMake - configuration error
item.description:
CMake - configuration error

CMake configuration errors occur when CMakeLists.txt has syntax errors, missing targets, or incompatible settings.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: Missing target
add_executable(myapp) # missing source files

# Cause 2: Wrong syntax
set(SOURCES main.cpp utils.cpp) # missing quotes for paths with spaces
add_executable(myapp ${SOURCES})

# Cause 3: Missing find_package
target_link_libraries(myapp Boost::system) # Boost not found

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check CMake syntax

cmake -S . -B build 2>&1 | head -50

Fix 2: Fix CMakeLists.txt

cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.16)
project(MyApp)

add_executable(myapp main.cpp utils.cpp)
target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE Boost::system)

Fix 3: Set CMake variables

cmake -S . -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug

Related Errors

  • Meson - build error — Meson build errors.
  • Ninja - build error — Ninja build errors.
  • Conan - package error — Conan errors.

item.title: [Solution] C++ Conan - package manager error
item.description:
Conan - package manager error

Conan errors occur during package installation, dependency resolution, or when packages fail to build.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: Package not found
[requires]
nonexistent/1.0 # recipe not in remote

# Cause 2: Version conflict
[requires]
boost/1.80.0
other-boost/1.70.0 # conflicting boost versions

# Cause 3: Binary compatibility
# Package built for different compiler/settings

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check available packages

conan search "boost/*" -r conancenter

Fix 2: Fix conanfile.txt

[requires]
boost/1.83.0

[generators]
cmake_find_package

Fix 3: Install and build

mkdir build && cd build
conan install .. --build=missing
cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build .

Related Errors

  • vcpkg - package error — vcpkg errors.
  • CMake - configuration error — CMake errors.
  • Meson - build error — Meson errors.

item.title: [Solution] C++ cppcheck - static analysis error
item.description:
cppcheck - static analysis error

cppcheck is a static analysis tool that finds bugs and dangerous coding patterns. It reports errors without running the code.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Memory leak
void func() {
 int* p = new int[100];
 if (error) return; // leak
 delete[] p;
}

// Cause 2: Uninitialized variable
void func() {
 int x;
 if (condition) x = 5;
 printf("%d", x); // may be uninitialized
}

// Cause 3: Null pointer dereference
void func(int* p) {
 *p = 10; // may be null
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Run cppcheck

cppcheck --enable=all src/

Fix 2: Fix reported issues

// Fix memory leak
void func() {
 int* p = new int[100];
 delete[] p; // always free
}

// Fix uninitialized
void func() {
 int x = 0; // initialize
 if (condition) x = 5;
}

Fix 3: Use inline suppression

// cppcheck-suppress memoryLeak
int* p = new int[100]; // intentionally leak for performance

Related Errors

  • clang-tidy - lint error — clang-tidy errors.
  • Valgrind - memory error — memory errors.
  • AddressSanitizer — runtime memory errors.

item.title: [Solution] C++ Meson - build error
item.description:
Meson - build error

Meson build errors occur when meson.build has configuration issues, dependency problems, or compilation failures.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: Missing dependency
project('myapp', 'cpp')
dep = dependency('nonexistent') # not found

# Cause 2: Wrong source files
executable('myapp', 'missing_file.cpp') # file not found

# Cause 3: Compiler not found
project('myapp', 'cpp') # no C++ compiler installed

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check Meson setup

meson setup builddir
# Check error output

Fix 2: Fix meson.build

project('myapp', 'cpp', version: '1.0')

sources = files('main.cpp', 'utils.cpp')
executable('myapp', sources)

Fix 3: Install dependencies

# On Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install build-essential meson ninja-build

Related Errors

  • CMake - configuration error — CMake errors.
  • Ninja - build error — Ninja errors.
  • Conan - package error — Conan errors.

item.title: [Solution] C++ Ninja - build error
item.description:
Ninja - build error

Ninja build errors occur when the build.ninja file has issues, dependencies are missing, or compilation commands fail.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: build.ninja not generated
ninja: error: loading 'build.ninja': No such file or directory

# Cause 2: Dependency not built
ninja: error: dependency 'lib/libfoo.a' not found

# Cause 3: Compilation failure
error: unknown type name 'string'

How to Fix

Fix 1: Generate build.ninja first

# With CMake
cmake -S . -B build -G Ninja
ninja -C build

# With Meson
meson setup builddir
ninja -C builddir

Fix 2: Fix compilation errors

#include <string> // missing include
std::string s = "hello";

Fix 3: Check Ninja version

ninja --version
# Ensure compatible with your build system

Related Errors

  • CMake - configuration error — CMake errors.
  • Meson - build error — Meson errors.
  • Conan - package error — Conan errors.

item.title: [Solution] C++ vcpkg - package error
item.description:
vcpkg - package error

vcpkg errors occur when packages fail to install, dependencies conflict, or triplet configuration is wrong.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Package not found in vcpkg
{
 "dependencies": ["nonexistent-package"]
}

// Cause 2: Triplet mismatch
// Building for x64 but package only has ARM

// Cause 3: Port version conflict
{
 "dependencies": [
 { "name": "boost", "version>=": "1.83.0" }
 ]
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Search for package

vcpkg search boost

Fix 2: Fix vcpkg.json

{
 "dependencies": [
 "boost-algorithm",
 "boost-system"
 ]
}

Fix 3: Install with triplet

vcpkg install boost --triplet x64-linux

Related Errors

  • Conan - package error — Conan errors.
  • CMake - configuration error — CMake errors.
  • Ninja - build error — Ninja errors.

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Contracts Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A Kotlin contracts error occurs when contract declarations are invalid. Contracts allow the compiler to understand the effects of functions, enabling smart casts and other optimizations.

Common Causes

  • Invalid contract syntax
  • Contract does not match function behavior
  • Using contracts in non-inline functions
  • Wrong effect type

How to Fix

// WRONG: Contract in non-inline function
fun process(value: Any?) {
 contract {
 returns() implies (value != null) // Error: not inline
 }
}

// CORRECT: Contract in inline function
inline fun process(value: Any?, block: () -> Unit) {
 contract {
 callsInPlace(block, InvocationKind.EXACTLY_ONCE)
 }
 block()
}

// WRONG: Contract does not match behavior
inline fun isNotEmpty(value: String?): Boolean {
 contract {
 returns(true) implies (value != null) // Wrong: may return false with null
 }
 return value != null && value.isNotEmpty()
}

// CORRECT: Contract matches behavior
inline fun isNotNull(value: Any?): Boolean {
 contract {
 returns(true) implies (value != null)
 }
 return value != null
}

Examples

// Example 1: Implies contract
inline fun requireNotNull(value: Any?): Boolean {
 contract {
 returns(true) implies (value != null)
 }
 return value != null
}

fun process(value: Any?) {
 if (requireNotNull(value)) {
 println(value.length) // Smart cast to non-null
 }
}

// Example 2: callsInPlace contract
inline fun run(block: () -> Unit) {
 contract {
 callsInPlace(block, InvocationKind.EXACTLY_ONCE)
 }
 block()
}

fun example() {
 val x: Int
 run { x = 5 } // Allowed due to contract
 println(x)
}

// Example 3: returns contract
inline fun <T> Result<T>.getOrNull(): T? {
 contract {
 returns(null) implies (this@getOrNull.isFailure)
 }
 return getOrNull()
}

Related Errors

  • Inline class error — inline class issue
  • Inline reified error — reified type issue
  • Expect actual error — expect/actual mismatch

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Expect/Actual Mismatch Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

An expect/actual mismatch error occurs when an expect declaration does not have a matching actual implementation in all target platforms. Kotlin Multiplatform uses expect/actual for platform-specific code.

Common Causes

  • Missing actual declaration
  • Signature mismatch between expect and actual
  • Wrong platform target
  • Missing dependency

How to Fix

// WRONG: Missing actual declaration
expect class Platform {
 fun getName(): String
}
// No actual implementation

// CORRECT: Provide actual implementation
// commonMain
expect class Platform {
 fun getName(): String
}

// androidMain
actual class Platform actual constructor() {
 actual fun getName(): String = "Android"
}

// iosMain
actual class Platform actual constructor() {
 actual fun getName(): String = "iOS"
}

// WRONG: Signature mismatch
expect fun getPlatformName(): String
actual fun getPlatformName(): Int // Wrong return type

// CORRECT: Match signatures
expect fun getPlatformName(): String
actual fun getPlatformName(): String = "Android"

Examples

// Example 1: Platform class
expect class Navigator {
 fun navigateTo(url: String)
}

// androidMain
actual class Navigator {
 actual fun navigateTo(url: String) {
 // Android implementation
 }
}

// Example 2: Platform function
expect fun getCurrentTime(): Long

// androidMain
actual fun getCurrentTime(): Long = System.currentTimeMillis()

// Example 3: Platform constant
expect val PLATFORM_NAME: String

// androidMain
actual val PLATFORM_NAME: String = "Android"

Related Errors

  • Inline reified error — reified type issue
  • Contracts error — contracts issue
  • Gradle Kotlin DSL error — Gradle error

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin No Value Passed for Parameter — Missing Argument Fix
item.description:
No Value Passed for Parameter — Missing Argument Fix

A “No value passed for parameter” error occurs at compile time when you call a function without providing all required arguments. Kotlin requires all non-default parameters to be explicitly provided.

Description

Kotlin functions can have required parameters (no default value) and optional parameters (with default values). If a parameter has no default value and you don’t provide it, the compiler reports this error.

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Reified Type Parameter Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A reified type parameter error occurs when using reified types incorrectly. Reified types preserve generic type information at runtime, but only work with inline functions.

Common Causes

  • Not in inline function
  • Using reified in non-inline context
  • Wrong type check at runtime
  • Missing reified modifier

How to Fix

// WRONG: Reified in non-inline function
fun <T> checkType(value: Any): Boolean {
 return value is T // Error: T is erased
}

// CORRECT: Use reified inline function
inline fun <reified T> checkType(value: Any): Boolean {
 return value is T // T preserved at runtime
}

// WRONG: Not using inline
fun <reified T> filter(list: List<Any>): List<T> {
 return list.filterIsInstance<T>() // Error: not inline
}

// CORRECT: Use inline
inline fun <reified T> filter(list: List<Any>): List<T> {
 return list.filterIsInstance<T>()
}

Examples

// Example 1: Type check
inline fun <reified T> isType(value: Any): Boolean {
 return value is T
}
isType<String>("hello") // true
isType<Int>("hello") // false

// Example 2: Filter by type
inline fun <reified T> List<Any>.filterType(): List<T> {
 return filterIsInstance<T>()
}
val mixed = listOf(1, "two", 3.0, "four")
val strings = mixed.filterType<String>() // ["two", "four"]

// Example 3: Intent extras
inline fun <reified T> Intent.getExtra(key: String): T? {
 return extras?.get(key) as? T
}

Related Errors

  • Inline class error — inline class issue
  • [Inline reified error] — reified type issue
  • Expect actual error — expect/actual mismatch

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Type Mismatch — Compile-Time Type Fix
item.description:
Type Mismatch — Compile-Time Type Fix

A type mismatch error occurs at compile time when Kotlin’s type system detects an incompatible type. This is a compile error, not a runtime exception, and must be fixed before the code can run.

Description

Kotlin has a strict type system that catches type errors during compilation. Type mismatch happens when you assign a value of one type to a variable of a different type without proper conversion.

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Typealias Resolution Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A typealias resolution error occurs when a typealias cannot be resolved. Typealiases provide alternative names for existing types but can fail due to wrong import or circular references.

Common Causes

  • Missing import
  • Circular typealias references
  • Wrong type name
  • Platform type issues

How to Fix

// WRONG: Circular typealias
typealias A = B
typealias B = A // Circular reference

// CORRECT: Break the cycle
typealias A = String
typealias B = Int

// WRONG: Missing import
// file1.kt
typealias UserList = List<User>

// file2.kt - missing import
val users: UserList = // Error: UserList not found

// CORRECT: Import typealias
import com.example.UserList
val users: UserList = listOf()

Examples

// Example 1: Basic typealias
typealias UserId = Long
typealias UserMap = Map<UserId, User>

fun getUser(id: UserId): User? = // ...

// Example 2: Typealias for function types
typealias Callback = (Result<String>) -> Unit

fun fetchData(callback: Callback) {
 callback(Result.success("data"))
}

// Example 3: Typealias for nested types
typealias Matrix = Array<DoubleArray>

Related Errors

  • Inline class error — inline class issue
  • [Unresolved reference error] — type not found
  • ClassCastException — type cast failed

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Unresolved Reference — Name Not Found Fix
item.description:
Unresolved Reference — Name Not Found Fix

An unresolved reference error occurs at compile time when Kotlin cannot find the referenced variable, function, class, or property. This is one of the most common compile errors in Kotlin.

Description

Kotlin must be able to resolve every name you use to a declared symbol. If the name doesn’t exist, isn’t imported, or isn’t visible, the compiler reports an unresolved reference error.

Common scenarios:

item.title: [Solution] Swift some Protocol Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A some Protocol error occurs when using opaque return types incorrectly. The some keyword tells the compiler to hide the concrete type while ensuring it’s the same type every time the function is called.

Common Causes

  • Returning different types from function
  • Missing protocol conformance
  • Using some where existential type is needed
  • Generic constraint violations

How to Fix

// WRONG: Different return types
func makeShape() -> some Shape {
 if condition {
 return Circle() // Circle
 } else {
 return Square() // Square - different type!
 }
}

// CORRECT: Same return type
func makeShape() -> some Shape {
 if condition {
 return Circle()
 } else {
 return Circle() // Same type
 }
}

// WRONG: Protocol not conformed
func makeAnimal() -> some Animal {
 return Rock() // Rock doesn't conform to Animal
}

// CORRECT: Ensure conformance
protocol Animal {}
struct Dog: Animal {}

func makeAnimal() -> some Animal {
 return Dog()
}

// WRONG: Using some when you need existential
func makeValue() -> some Any {
 // May need different types
}

// CORRECT: Use Any or AnyProtocol
func makeValue() -> Any {
 return "Hello"
}

Examples

// Example 1: Basic some Protocol
protocol Drawable {
 func draw() -> String
}

struct Line: Drawable {
 func draw() -> String { "Line" }
}

func makeDrawable() -> some Drawable {
 return Line()
}

// Example 2: some with generics
func first<T: Comparable>(_ a: T, _ b: T) -> some Comparable {
 return a < b ? a : b
}

// Example 3: some in SwiftUI
var body: some View {
 VStack {
 Text("Hello")
 Image(systemName: "star")
 }
}

Related Errors

  • Opaque return type error — opaque type issue
  • Result type error — Result type issue
  • Throws error — error throwing issue

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1002 — Unterminated string literal
item.description:
TS1002 — Unterminated string literal

TS1002 occurs when a string literal is not properly closed with a matching quote. This prevents TypeScript from parsing the rest of the file.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing closing quote
const msg = "Hello, world; // TS1002

// Cause 2: Unescaped quote inside string
const quote = "She said "hello""; // TS1002

// Cause 3: String spanning lines without template literal
const multi = "Line 1
Line 2"; // TS1002

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add closing quote

const msg = "Hello, world";

Fix 2: Escape inner quotes

const quote = "She said \"hello\"";
// or use single quotes
const quote = 'She said "hello"';

Fix 3: Use template literals for multiline

const multi = `Line 1
Line 2`;

Related Errors

  • TS1361: Unterminated template literal — template literal variant.
  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon issues.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader syntax error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1005 — ';' expected
item.description:
TS1005 — ‘;’ expected

TS1005 is a syntax error indicating TypeScript expected a semicolon at the specified position. Missing semicolons, malformed statements, or incorrect syntax trigger this error.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing semicolon after statement
let x = 5
let y = 10 // TS1005: ';' expected before 'let'

// Cause 2: Missing semicolon in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++)
 console.log(i) // missing semicolon in some contexts

// Cause 3: Wrong syntax in import
import { name } from "./module"
import { other } from "./other" // may trigger TS1005

// Cause 4: Missing semicolon in type definition
interface User {
 name: string
 age: number // TS1005
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add missing semicolons

let x = 5;
let y = 10;

Fix 2: Check semicolon context

// Semicolons required after:
let x = 5;
const y = "hello";
function foo() { return 1; }

Fix 3: Enable auto-semicolons in editor

Configure your editor to insert semicolons automatically.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1005 — Semicolon Expected
item.description:
TS1005 — ‘;’ expected

TS1005 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected a semicolon. This usually means there’s a syntax issue with how statements, declarations, or expressions are structured.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing semicolon after variable declaration
let x = 10
let y = 20 // TS1005 on this line — expected ';' after declaration

// Cause 2: Missing semicolon in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++)
{ // TS1005 — expected ';' after for statement

// Cause 3: Missing semicolon after interface property
interface User {
 name: string
 age: number // TS1005 if strict semicolon enforcement
}

// Cause 4: Missing semicolon in import statement
import { helper } from "./utils"
import { other } from "./other" // TS1005

Solutions

Fix 1: Add missing semicolons

// Wrong
let x = 10
let y = 20

// Correct
let x = 10;
let y = 20;

Fix 2: Use a linter to enforce semicolons

// .eslintrc.json
{
 "rules": {
 "semi": ["error", "always"]
 }
}

Fix 3: Check for missing closing brackets or braces

// Wrong — missing closing parenthesis
const result = add(1, 2
// TS1005: expected ';' — really means missing ')'

// Correct
const result = add(1, 2);

Fix 4: Fix malformed expressions

// Wrong — double operator
let x = 10 ++ 5; // TS1005

// Correct
let x = 10 + 5;

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally using a keyword
let class = "myClass"; // TS1005: ';' expected (class is reserved)

// Example 2: Forgetting semicolon in chained method
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name) // OK with ASI
 .join(", "); // TS1005 if context requires semicolon

// Fix: add semicolon at end
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name)
 .join(", ");

Related Errors

  • TS1109: Expression expected — related parse error.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected token.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1009 — Declaration or statement expected
item.description:
TS1009 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1009 is a parser-level syntax error indicating TypeScript expected a declaration or statement at a position where it found something else. This is typically caused by malformed code structure.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extra comma in declaration list
let x, y, , z; // TS1009

// Cause 2: Invalid token after import
import from "module"; // TS1009: missing import specifiers

// Cause 3: Unexpected token in interface
interface User {
 name: string,
 , // TS1009
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Remove extra commas

let x, y, z;

Fix 2: Fix import syntax

import { something } from "module";
// or
import defaultExport from "module";

Fix 3: Fix interface declaration

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

Related Errors

  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — similar parser error.
  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon expected.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — expression expected.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a syntax error indicating TypeScript expected an expression at a position where none was found. This typically happens with malformed function calls, object literals, or template literals.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing argument in function call
doSomething(); // if function expects args and comma is missing
doSomething(, "hello"); // TS1109: expression expected before comma

// Cause 2: Empty template literal issue
const msg = `Hello, ${}`; // TS1109: expression expected inside ${}

// Cause 3: Invalid object syntax
const obj = {
 name: "Alice"
 age: 30 // TS1109 if comma missing
};

// Cause 4: Malformed ternary
const x = true ? ; // TS1109: expression expected after ?

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check for missing expressions

// Remove extra commas
doSomething("hello");

// Fill in template literal
const msg = `Hello, ${name}`;

Fix 2: Add missing commas in objects

const obj = {
 name: "Alice",
 age: 30
};

Fix 3: Complete ternary expressions

const x = true ? "yes" : "no";

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — missing semicolon.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — missing declaration.
  • TS1136: Expected block of statements — missing block.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression Expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected an expression. This usually happens with malformed function calls, missing operands, or incomplete code.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Empty function call argument
const result = Math.random() // Missing closing paren or argument

// Cause 2: Missing right operand
let x = 5 + ; // TS1109: expression expected after '+'

// Cause 3: Incomplete arrow function
const fn = () => // TS1109: expression expected after '=>'

// Cause 4: Missing value in assignment
const obj = {
 key: // TS1109: expression expected after ':'
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Complete the expression

// Wrong
let x = 5 + ;

// Correct
let x = 5 + 10;

Fix 2: Close parentheses and provide arguments

// Wrong
const result = Math.random()

// Correct
const result = Math.random();

Fix 3: Provide a return value for arrow functions

// Wrong
const fn = () =>

// Correct
const fn = () => 42;

// Or with block body
const fn = () => {
 return 42;
};

Fix 4: Complete object property values

// Wrong
const obj = {
 key:
};

// Correct
const obj = {
 key: "value"
};

Examples

// Example 1: Incomplete template literal
const name = `Hello, ${; // TS1109

// Fix:
const name = `Hello, ${user.name}`;

// Example 2: Missing argument in new expression
const el = new HTMLElement(); // If HTMLElement isn't defined
// TS1109 if the expression can't be resolved

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader token error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected
item.description:
TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1128 occurs when TypeScript encounters an unexpected token at a position where a declaration or statement is expected. This is a broad syntax error that can appear in many contexts.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Stray characters outside functions
const x = 5;
} // TS1128: stray closing brace

// Cause 2: Extra closing parenthesis
const arr = [1, 2, 3]); // TS1128

// Cause 3: Invalid top-level expression
5 + 3; // OK in some contexts, but in type file...

// Cause 4: Missing body after declaration
function foo(); // TS1128 if not in ambient declaration

How to Fix

Fix 1: Remove stray characters

const x = 5;
// no stray braces

Fix 2: Check bracket matching

const arr = [1, 2, 3]; // correct brackets

Fix 3: Use declare for ambient declarations

declare function foo(): void; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon expected.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — expression expected.
  • TS1136: Expected block of statements — block expected.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1128 — Declaration or Statement Expected
item.description:
TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1128 occurs when TypeScript encounters a token where it expects a declaration (variable, function, class, etc.) or a statement. This often indicates a structural syntax problem like extra closing braces, misplaced code, or invalid top-level constructs.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extra closing brace
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}} // TS1128: extra '}' — declaration or statement expected

// Cause 2: Code outside of a function or class at invalid location
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // TS1128: unexpected indentation / misplaced code

// Cause 3: Missing function body or expression
function compute() // TS1128: expected '{' or '=>'

// Cause 4: Invalid top-level expression
42 // TS1128 if not in a module or script context properly

Solutions

Fix 1: Remove extra closing braces

// Wrong
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}}

// Correct
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}

Fix 2: Ensure code is inside proper blocks

// Wrong
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // outside the function

// Correct
function process() {
 const x = 10; // inside the function
}

Fix 3: Complete function declarations

// Wrong
function compute()

// Correct
function compute(): number {
 return 42;
}

Fix 4: Check for misplaced imports or exports

// Wrong — export outside module context
export default {} // TS1128 if file is not a module

// Correct — ensure the file has at least one import/export
import { something } from "./other";
export default {};

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally closing a block early
if (condition) {
 doSomething();
} // Extra close
doOtherThing(); // TS1128

// Example 2: Mixing declaration and expression
const obj = {
 method() {}
}
const x = 10 // Missing semicolon, may cause TS1128 on next line

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — incomplete expressions.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1136 — Expected block of statements
item.description:
TS1136 — Expected block of statements

TS1136 occurs when TypeScript expects a block of statements (wrapped in {}) but finds something else instead. This typically happens with control structures missing their body braces.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing braces after if
if (condition)
 doSomething(); // TS1136 in some parser contexts

// Cause 2: Empty block
if (condition) {
 // TS1136 if body is expected but empty
}

// Cause 3: Wrong syntax for function body
function foo() // TS1136: expected block

How to Fix

Fix 1: Always use braces for blocks

if (condition) {
 doSomething();
}

Fix 2: Provide function body

function foo() {
 return 42;
}

Fix 3: Use empty block explicitly

if (condition) {
 // intentionally empty
}

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon issues.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — expression expected.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — declaration expected.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1361 — Unterminated template literal
item.description:
TS1361 — Unterminated template literal

TS1361 occurs when a template literal (backtick string) is not properly closed with a matching backtick. This is a syntax error that prevents TypeScript from parsing the file.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing closing backtick
const msg = `Hello, world; // TS1361

// Cause 2: Missing closing backtick after expression
const name = "Alice";
const greeting = `Hello, ${name}; // TS1361

// Cause 3: Unclosed multiline template
const html = `
 <div>
 <h1>Title</h1>
 </div> // TS1361 if closing backtick missing
`;

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add closing backtick

const msg = `Hello, world`;

Fix 2: Ensure expression blocks are complete

const greeting = `Hello, ${name}`;

Fix 3: Check multiline templates

const html = `
 <div>
 <h1>Title</h1>
 </div>
`;

Related Errors

  • TS1002: Unterminated string literal — regular string variant.
  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon issues.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader syntax error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1434 — Unexpected Keyword or Identifier
item.description:
TS1434 — Unexpected keyword or identifier

TS1434 occurs when TypeScript encounters a keyword or identifier in a position where it doesn’t belong according to the grammar rules. This is typically caused by using reserved words in invalid contexts or placing identifiers where expressions or declarations are expected.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a reserved keyword as an identifier
let class = "myClass"; // TS1434: Unexpected keyword 'class'

// Cause 2: Misplaced 'return' outside a function
return 42; // TS1434 if at top level

// Cause 3: Using 'type' as a variable name (pre-5.0)
type = 10; // TS1434: 'type' is a reserved keyword in TypeScript

// Cause 4: Keyword in wrong position in type annotation
type Foo = public string; // TS1434: 'public' not valid here

Solutions

Fix 1: Rename the identifier to avoid reserved words

// Wrong
let class = "myClass";

// Correct
let className = "myClass";

Fix 2: Move return statements inside functions

// Wrong
return 42;

// Correct
function getValue() {
 return 42;
}

Fix 3: Use different variable names for reserved words

// Wrong — 'type' is reserved in TypeScript
let type = "foo";

// Correct
let typeValue = "foo";

Fix 4: Remove invalid modifiers from type annotations

// Wrong
type Foo = public string;

// Correct — access modifiers are for class members, not type aliases
type Foo = string;

Examples

// Example 1: Using 'const' in wrong context
interface Config {
 const host: string; // TS1434: 'const' not valid in interface
}

// Fix: remove 'const'
interface Config {
 host: string;
}

// Example 2: Using 'async' outside function
async const result = fetch("/api"); // TS1434

// Fix: use async function or async IIFE
const result = (async () => fetch("/api"))();

Related Errors

  • TS1435: Unknown keyword or identifier — unrecognized keyword.
  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — related parse error.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1435 — Unknown Keyword or Identifier
item.description:
TS1435 — Unknown keyword or identifier

TS1435 occurs when TypeScript encounters an identifier that it doesn’t recognize as a valid keyword or identifier in the current context. This often means a typo in a keyword, using a future/reserved word, or an unrecognized syntax.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in a keyword
const x = 10;
pritn(x); // TS1435: 'pritn' is not recognized (typo of 'print')

// Cause 2: Using a keyword from another language
def greet(): // TS1435: 'def' is not a TypeScript keyword (Python syntax)

// Cause 3: Unrecognized decorator or modifier
@sealed // TS1435 if decorator not recognized or not enabled
class MyClass {}

// Cause 4: Misspelled type annotation keyword
let x: numberr = 10; // TS1435: 'numberr' is not a valid type

Solutions

Fix 1: Correct keyword typos

// Wrong
pritn(x);

// Correct
console.log(x);

Fix 2: Use valid TypeScript keywords

// Wrong — Python syntax
def greet():

// Correct — TypeScript function
function greet(): void {}

Fix 3: Enable decorator support if needed

// tsconfig.json
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "experimentalDecorators": true,
 "emitDecoratorMetadata": true
 }
}

Fix 4: Fix type annotation typos

// Wrong
let x: numberr = 10;

// Correct
let x: number = 10;

Examples

// Example 1: Using a non-existent type
let data: Arrray<number> = []; // TS1435: 'Arrray' not recognized

// Fix:
let data: Array<number> = [];

// Example 2: Wrong loop syntax from another language
for i in range(10) { // TS1435: not valid TypeScript
}

// Fix:
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {}

Related Errors

  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — keyword in wrong position.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS18046 — 'X' Is of Type 'unknown'
item.description:
TS18046 — ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’

TS18046 occurs when you try to use a value typed as unknown without first narrowing its type. The unknown type is TypeScript’s type-safe alternative to any — you must verify the type before accessing properties or methods.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Catch clause variable is unknown by default
try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 console.log(e.message); // TS18046: 'e' is of type 'unknown'
}

// Cause 2: Using unknown from a generic
function parse<T>(input: unknown): T {
 return input; // TS18046 if you try to access input properties
}

// Cause 3: Fetching untyped data
const response = await fetch("/api");
const data = await response.json(); // data is 'any', but if typed as 'unknown'...
console.log(data.name); // TS18046

// Cause 4: Value from a Map with unknown value type
const map = new Map<string, unknown>();
const val = map.get("key");
console.log(val.toString()); // TS18046

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a type guard to narrow the type

// Wrong
try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 console.log(e.message); // TS18046
}

// Correct
try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 if (e instanceof Error) {
 console.log(e.message); // OK — e is Error
 }
}

Fix 2: Use typeof checks for primitives

function process(value: unknown) {
 if (typeof value === "string") {
 console.log(value.toUpperCase()); // OK — value is string
 }
}

Fix 3: Use type assertions when you know the type

const data = await response.json() as User;
console.log(data.name); // OK

Fix 4: Use a validated parsing library

import { z } from "zod";

const UserSchema = z.object({
 name: z.string(),
 age: z.number(),
});

const data = UserSchema.parse(await response.json());
console.log(data.name); // OK — typed as { name: string; age: number }

Examples

// Example 1: forEach with unknown
const items: unknown[] = [1, "hello", true];
items.forEach(item => {
 console.log(item.toFixed()); // TS18046
});

// Fix:
items.forEach(item => {
 if (typeof item === "number") {
 console.log(item.toFixed()); // OK
 }
});

// Example 2: JSON.parse returns any, but explicit unknown needs narrowing
const parsed: unknown = JSON.parse('{"x": 10}');
console.log(parsed.x); // TS18046

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — the opposite: no type at all vs strict unknown.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability issues.
  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — similar narrowing needed for null.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS18046 — Variable is of type 'unknown'
item.description:
TS18046 — Variable is of type ‘unknown’

TS18046 occurs when you try to use a value of type unknown in a way that requires a more specific type. unknown is the type-safe counterpart of any — you must narrow it before use.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: catch clause variable is unknown
try {
 throw new Error("oops");
} catch (e) {
 console.log(e.message); // TS18046: e is 'unknown'
}

// Cause 2: JSON.parse returns unknown
const data = JSON.parse('{"name": "Alice"}');
console.log(data.name); // TS18046

// Cause 3: Function returning unknown
function parse(input: string): unknown {
 return JSON.parse(input);
}
const result = parse("{}");
result.foo; // TS18046

How to Fix

Fix 1: Type guard

try {
 throw new Error("oops");
} catch (e) {
 if (e instanceof Error) {
 console.log(e.message); // OK
 }
}

Fix 2: Type assertion

const data = JSON.parse('{"name": "Alice"}') as { name: string };
console.log(data.name);

Fix 3: Define a return type

function parse(input: string): { name: string } {
 return JSON.parse(input);
}

Fix 4: Use a validation library

import { z } from "zod";
const schema = z.object({ name: z.string() });
const data = schema.parse(JSON.parse(input));

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — any type instead of unknown.
  • TS2571: Object is of type ‘unknown’ — object unknown variant.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — unresolved identifier.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS18047 — X is possibly 'null'
item.description:
TS18047 — ‘X’ is possibly ’null’

TS18047 occurs when you access a property or call a method on a variable that TypeScript knows might be null.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Nullable variable
let name: string | null = getNullableName();
console.log(name.length); // TS18047

// Cause 2: DOM element might be null
const el = document.getElementById("myId");
el.innerHTML = "hello"; // TS18047

// Cause 3: Function returning null
function find(id: number): User | null {
 return db.users.find(u => u.id === id) ?? null;
}
const user = find(1);
console.log(user.name); // TS18047

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add null check

if (name !== null) {
 console.log(name.length);
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

console.log(name?.length);

Fix 3: Use non-null assertion

console.log(name!.length);

Fix 4: Default value

console.log((name ?? "").length);

Related Errors

  • TS18048: X is possibly ‘undefined’ — undefined variant.
  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — property access variant.
  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — undefined variant.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS18048 — X is possibly 'undefined'
item.description:
TS18048 — ‘X’ is possibly ‘undefined’

TS18048 occurs when you access a property or call a method on a variable that TypeScript knows might be undefined.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Variable with undefined union
let name: string | undefined = getName();
console.log(name.length); // TS18048

// Cause 2: Object property that might be undefined
interface Config {
 db?: { host: string };
}
const config: Config = {};
console.log(config.db.host); // TS18048

// Cause 3: Map.get returns undefined
const map = new Map<string, number>();
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed(); // TS18048

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add undefined check

if (name !== undefined) {
 console.log(name.length);
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

console.log(name?.length);

Fix 3: Use non-null assertion

console.log(name!.length);

Fix 4: Default value

const val = map.get("key") ?? 0;

Related Errors

  • TS18047: X is possibly ’null’ — null variant.
  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — property access variant.
  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — null variant.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2300 — Duplicate identifier
item.description:
TS2300 — Duplicate identifier

TS2300 occurs when two declarations in the same scope have the same name. This can happen with variables, functions, classes, or type declarations.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Duplicate variable declaration
let x = 5;
let x = 10; // TS2300: Duplicate identifier 'x'

// Cause 2: Duplicate function declaration
function greet() {}
function greet() {} // TS2300

// Cause 3: Conflicting type declarations
interface Config {
 port: number;
}
interface Config {
 host: string; // TS2300: duplicate identifier
}

// Cause 4: Conflicting with global type
let Array = [1, 2, 3]; // TS2300: 'Array' already defined

How to Fix

Fix 1: Rename one of the declarations

let x1 = 5;
let x2 = 10;

Fix 2: Use unique names or modules

// In separate modules
// file1.ts
export const config1 = {};

// file2.ts
export const config2 = {};

Fix 3: Merge interface declarations

interface Config {
 port: number;
 host: string;
}

Fix 4: Avoid shadowing globals

const myArray = [1, 2, 3]; // use different name

Related Errors

  • TS2309: Variable is declared but never read — unused declaration.
  • TS2391: Function redeclaration — function redeclaration.
  • TS2464: Circular definition of import alias — circular imports.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2300 — Duplicate Identifier
item.description:
TS2300 — Duplicate identifier

TS2300 occurs when the same identifier is declared more than once in the same scope. TypeScript doesn’t allow redeclaring a variable, parameter, or type name that already exists in the current block or file.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Redeclaring a variable in the same scope
let x = 10;
let x = 20; // TS2300: Duplicate identifier 'x'

// Cause 2: Parameter name conflicts with variable
let name = "Alice";
function greet(name: string) {} // TS2304 if 'name' is in outer scope with let/const

// Cause 3: Duplicate interface declarations
interface Config {
 host: string;
}
interface Config { // TS2300: Duplicate identifier
 port: number;
}

// Cause 4: Importing the same name from different modules
import { helper } from "./a";
import { helper } from "./b"; // TS2304 if both export 'helper'

Solutions

Fix 1: Rename the duplicate identifier

// Wrong
let x = 10;
let x = 20;

// Correct
let x = 10;
let x2 = 20;

Fix 2: Use block scoping to avoid conflicts

let name = "Alice";

function greet() {
 let name = "Bob"; // OK — different scope
 console.log(name);
}

Fix 3: Merge interface declarations

// Wrong
interface Config {
 host: string;
}
interface Config {
 port: number;
}

// Correct — merge into one
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}

Fix 4: Rename imports to avoid conflicts

import { helper as helperA } from "./a";
import { helper as helperB } from "./b";

Examples

// Example 1: Redeclaring in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {}
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {} // TS2300: 'i' already declared

// Fix: use different variable names
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {}
for (let j = 0; j < 5; j++) {}

// Example 2: Conflicting class and namespace
class MyClass {}
namespace MyClass {} // TS2300 in some configurations

Related Errors

  • TS2301: Duplicate function implementation — duplicate function bodies.
  • TS2309: An export assignment cannot be used in a module — export syntax conflicts.
  • TS2464: A class can only implement an identifier — related identifier issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2301 — Duplicate Function Implementation
item.description:
TS2301 — Duplicate function implementation

TS2301 occurs when the same function is implemented more than once in the same scope. This differs from function overloads, which require different parameter signatures. Duplicate implementations mean the exact same signature appears twice.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Defining the same function twice
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
function greet(name: string) { // TS2301: Duplicate function implementation
 return `Hi, ${name}`;
}

// Cause 2: Arrow function reassigned in same scope
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b; // TS2301

// Cause 3: Method defined twice in a class
class Service {
 process() { return 1; }
 process() { return 2; } // TS2301
}

// Cause 4: Duplicate function in module augmentation
declare module "foo" {
 function bar(): void;
 function bar(): string; // TS2301 — same signature
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Remove the duplicate implementation

// Wrong — two implementations
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hi, ${name}`;
}

// Correct — keep only one
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}

Fix 2: Use function overloads if you need multiple signatures

// Wrong — same signature twice
function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: string): string; // TS2301

// Correct — different signatures
function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: number): string;
function process(input: string | number): string {
 return String(input);
}

Fix 3: Rename duplicate arrow functions

// Wrong
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;

// Correct — use a different name
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;
const addVerbose = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;

Fix 4: Merge method implementations

// Wrong
class Service {
 process() { return 1; }
 process() { return 2; }
}

// Correct
class Service {
 process(mode?: string) {
 return mode === "fast" ? 2 : 1;
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Accidental duplicate in module
// utils.ts
export function formatDate(d: Date) { return d.toISOString(); }
export function formatDate(d: Date) { return d.toLocaleDateString(); } // TS2301

// Example 2: Namespace and function conflict
function build() {}
namespace build {} // May cause TS2301 in some configurations

Related Errors

  • TS2300: Duplicate identifier — duplicate variable/type names.
  • TS2309: An export assignment cannot be used in a module — export syntax issues.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload resolution failures.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2304 — Cannot Find Name
item.description:
TS2304 — Cannot Find Name

TS2304 occurs when TypeScript encounters an identifier that has not been declared in the current scope. This typically means a variable, function, class, or type is being used without being imported or defined.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a variable before declaring it
console.log(x); // TS2304: Cannot find name 'x'

// Cause 2: Missing import for a type or value
const data: User = {}; // TS2304: Cannot find name 'User'

// Cause 3: Typo in variable or function name
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2304: Cannot find name 'useName'

// Cause 4: Using a global without the proper lib or type
const result = fetch("/api"); // TS2304: Cannot find name 'fetch' if lib not configured

Solutions

Fix 1: Declare the variable before using it

// Wrong
console.log(x);

// Correct
let x = 10;
console.log(x);

Fix 2: Import missing types and modules

// Wrong — User type is not imported
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

// Correct
import { User } from "./models";
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

Fix 3: Fix typos in identifiers

// Wrong
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2304

// Correct
console.log(userName);

Fix 4: Add missing lib references in tsconfig

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "lib": ["ES2020", "DOM"]
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Forgetting to import
// file: app.ts
const arr = new Array(); // TS2304 if Array is not imported

// Example 2: Using a global type without @types
const el = document.getElementById("app"); // TS2304 if DOM types are missing

// Example 3: Circular dependency causing unresolved names
// a.ts
import { b } from "./b";
export const a = b + 1;

// b.ts
import { a } from "./a";
export const b = a + 1; // May produce TS2304

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module import path is wrong or missing.
  • TS2552: Cannot find name, did you mean… — similar but offers a close match.
  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — related to missing type annotations.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2304 — Cannot find name 'X'
item.description:
TS2304 — Cannot find name ‘X’

TS2304 occurs when TypeScript encounters a name it cannot resolve. This typically means a variable, function, class, or type is used without being declared or imported.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using undeclared variable
console.log(myVar); // TS2304: Cannot find name 'myVar'

// Cause 2: Missing import
// file: app.ts
greet("Alice"); // TS2304 if greet is defined in utils.ts but not imported

// Cause 3: Missing global type definition
console.log(process.env.NODE_ENV); // TS2304 if @types/node not installed

// Cause 4: Typo in name
const userName = "Alice";
console.log(userNme); // TS2304: typo

How to Fix

Fix 1: Declare the variable

const myVar = "hello";
console.log(myVar);

Fix 2: Import the function

import { greet } from "./utils";
greet("Alice");

Fix 3: Install type definitions

npm install --save-dev @types/node

Fix 4: Add global type declaration

// global.d.ts
declare const myGlobal: string;

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.
  • TS2552: Cannot find name - did you mean — similar name suggestions.
  • TS2503: Cannot find namespace — namespace not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2304 v2 — Cannot Find Name Fix
item.description:
TS2304 — Cannot Find Name (v2)

This variant covers TS2304 errors involving missing global declarations, environment variables, DOM APIs not in lib, and names from packages without type support.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2304: Cannot find name 'process'
  • TS2304: Cannot find name 'require'
  • TS2304: Cannot find name '__dirname'
  • TS2304: Cannot find name 'AbortController'
  • TS2304: Cannot find name 'myGlobal'

TypeScript cannot find a declaration for the identifier. This means it’s not imported, not declared in the current scope, and not available in the global type definitions.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2305 — Module 'X' has no exported member 'Y'
item.description:
TS2305 — Module ‘“X”’ has no exported member ‘Y’

TS2305 occurs when you import a member from a module that does not export that name. The member may not be exported, may not exist, or the module may have changed its API.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Importing non-exported member
// file: utils.ts
function helper() {} // not exported

// file: app.ts
import { helper } from "./utils"; // TS2305

// Cause 2: Wrong package version
import { newFeature } from "some-package"; // TS2305 if not in installed version

// Cause 3: Typo in member name
import { procesor } from "./cpu"; // TS2305: should be 'processor'

How to Fix

Fix 1: Export the member

// file: utils.ts
export function helper() {}

Fix 2: Check package version

npm ls some-package
# Update if needed
npm install some-package@latest

Fix 3: Fix the import name

import { processor } from "./cpu";

Fix 4: Use default import

import utils from "./utils";
utils.helper();

Related Errors

  • TS2614: Module has no exported member — similar variant.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.
  • TS2694: Namespace has no exported member — namespace variant.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2306 — File 'X' is not a module
item.description:
TS2306 — File ‘X’ is not a module

TS2306 occurs when you try to import from a file that does not have any top-level export or import statements. Without these, TypeScript treats the file as a script rather than a module.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: File has no exports
// file: utils.ts
function helper() {
 return 42;
}
// no 'export' keyword

// file: app.ts
import { helper } from "./utils"; // TS2306

// Cause 2: Using CommonJS without module flag
// file: old.js
module.exports = { helper };
// TypeScript may not recognize this as module

// Cause 3: Empty file
// file: empty.ts
// file: app.ts
import "./empty"; // TS2306

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add export statements

// file: utils.ts
export function helper() {
 return 42;
}

Fix 2: Add a re-export

// file: utils.ts
const helper = () => 42;
export { helper };

Fix 3: Use export default

// file: utils.ts
export default function helper() {
 return 42;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2707: X is a type but not a module — type-only file.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.
  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — type-only usage.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2307 — Cannot Find Module
item.description:
TS2307 — Cannot Find Module

TS2307 occurs when TypeScript cannot resolve a module imported via import or require(). This can happen when the module path is incorrect, the module doesn’t exist, or type declarations are missing.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in module path
import { helper } from "./helpr"; // TS2307: Cannot find module './helpr'

// Cause 2: Missing type declarations for a package
import _ from "lodash"; // TS2307 if @types/lodash is not installed

// Cause 3: Module not installed via npm
import express from "express"; // TS2307 if express is not in node_modules

// Cause 4: Incorrect relative path
import { utils } from "../utils"; // TS2307 if the path doesn't resolve

Solutions

Fix 1: Correct the import path

// Wrong
import { helper } from "./helpr";

// Correct
import { helper } from "./helper";

Fix 2: Install missing type declarations

npm install --save-dev @types/lodash
npm install --save-dev @types/express

Fix 3: Install the missing package

npm install express

Fix 4: Configure module resolution in tsconfig

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "moduleResolution": "node",
 "baseUrl": ".",
 "paths": {
 "@app/*": ["src/*"]
 }
 }
}

Fix 5: Create a type declaration file for untyped modules

// types/custom-module.d.ts
declare module "untyped-module" {
 const value: any;
 export default value;
}

Examples

// Example 1: Missing @types package
import jwt from "jsonwebtoken";
// TS2307: Cannot find module 'jsonwebtoken' or its corresponding type declarations
// Fix: npm install --save-dev @types/jsonwebtoken

// Example 2: Incorrect relative path
// src/utils.ts exists but you import from wrong path
import { formatDate } from "./util"; // TS2307 — file is "utils", not "util"

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope, unrelated to module resolution.
  • TS2309: An export assignment cannot be used in a module — export syntax mismatch.
  • TS7016: Could not find a declaration file for module — module exists but has no types.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2307 — Cannot find module 'X'
item.description:
TS2307 — Cannot find module ‘X’

TS2307 occurs when TypeScript cannot resolve an import to a module. This can happen because the module is not installed, the path is wrong, or type definitions are missing.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Module not installed
import express from "express"; // TS2307 if express not installed

// Cause 2: Wrong import path
import { helper } from "./utils"; // TS2307 if file is utils.ts in different directory

// Cause 3: Missing type definitions
import lodash from "lodash"; // TS2307 if @types/lodash not installed

// Cause 4: Relative path without extension
import { foo } from "./bar"; // TS2307 if bar.js (not .ts)

How to Fix

Fix 1: Install the package

npm install express

Fix 2: Install type definitions

npm install --save-dev @types/express

Fix 3: Fix the import path

import { helper } from "../utils"; // correct relative path

Fix 4: Configure moduleResolution in tsconfig

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "moduleResolution": "node"
 }
}

Examples

# Find where TypeScript looks for modules
npx tsc --traceResolution

# Check installed packages
npm ls express

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2306: File is not a module — file found but not a module.
  • TS2503: Cannot find namespace — namespace not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2307 v2 — Cannot Find Module Fix
item.description:
TS2307 — Cannot Find Module (v2)

This variant of TS2307 covers module resolution failures involving path aliases, relative imports, missing type declarations, and mismatches between ESM and CJS configurations.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2307: Cannot find module '@utils/helper' or its corresponding type declarations
  • TS2307: Cannot find module './types' or its corresponding type declarations
  • TS2307: Cannot find module 'my-lib' because it is not declared in the 'typesVersions' field
  • TS2307: Cannot find module '@/components/Button'

TypeScript uses the moduleResolution strategy in tsconfig.json to find imported modules. Path aliases, missing type packages, or incorrect resolution strategies cause this error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2309 — Export Assignment Cannot Be Used in a Module
item.description:
TS2309 — An export assignment cannot be used in a module

TS2309 occurs when you use export = (CommonJS-style default export) in a file that TypeScript treats as an ES module. This happens when your tsconfig’s module setting is es2015/esnext but you’re using CommonJS export syntax.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using 'export =' in ES module mode
export = {
 name: "myModule",
 version: "1.0.0",
}; // TS2309 if module is ES2015+

// Cause 2: Mixing 'export =' with named exports
export = function () {};
export const other = 1; // TS2309 — can't mix with export =

// Cause 3: 'export =' in a declaration file designed for CommonJS
// types/my-lib.d.ts
declare function myLib(): void;
export = myLib; // TS2309 if consuming file uses ES modules

// Cause 4: Importing with 'import =' when file uses export default
import myLib = require("my-lib"); // TS2309 if module resolution is ES

Solutions

Fix 1: Use export default instead of export =

// Wrong — CommonJS style
export = {
 name: "myModule",
};

// Correct — ES module style
export default {
 name: "myModule",
};

Fix 2: Use named exports

// Wrong
export = {
 name: "myModule",
 version: "1.0.0",
};

// Correct
export const name = "myModule";
export const version = "1.0.0";

Fix 3: Switch to CommonJS module resolution

// tsconfig.json
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "module": "commonjs"
 }
}

Fix 4: Use import/export syntax compatible with your module setting

// For ES modules
import myLib from "my-lib";

// For CommonJS
import myLib = require("my-lib");

Examples

// Example 1: Declaration file with export =
// my-lib.d.ts
declare class MyClass {
 constructor();
 doSomething(): void;
}
export = MyClass; // Only works with "module": "commonjs"

// Example 2: Trying to use both export = and export default
export = something;
export default something; // TS2309 — can't have both

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module import path issues.
  • TS2300: Duplicate identifier — duplicate exports.
  • TS2749: Refers to a value, used as a type — value vs type confusion.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2309 — Variable 'X' is declared but its value is never read
item.description:
TS2309 — Variable ‘X’ is declared but its value is never read

TS2309 occurs when noUnusedLocals is enabled and a variable is declared but never used. This is a code quality check to reduce dead code.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Imported but unused
import { helper } from "./utils"; // TS2309 if helper is never used

// Cause 2: Local variable never read
function process() {
 const temp = calculate(); // TS2309 if temp is never used
 return 42;
}

// Cause 3: Destructured but unused
const { name, age } = user; // TS2309 if age is never used

How to Fix

Fix 1: Remove the unused import

// Remove: import { helper } from "./utils";

Fix 2: Use the variable

function process() {
 const temp = calculate();
 console.log(temp); // now used
 return 42;
}

Fix 3: Prefix with underscore

const { name, _age } = user; // underscore prefix suppresses warning

Fix 4: Disable the check

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "noUnusedLocals": false
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS6133: Variable is declared but never used — parameter variant.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload issues.
  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — null access.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2320 — Interface 'X' cannot extend 'Y'
item.description:
TS2320 — Interface ‘X’ cannot extend ‘Y’

TS2320 occurs when an interface tries to extend a type that cannot be extended, such as a primitive type, a class with private members, or an incompatible type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extending a primitive type
interface MyString extends string { // TS2320
 toUpperCase(): string;
}

// Cause 2: Extending a class with private members
class Secret {
 private data: string;
 constructor() { this.data = "secret"; }
}

interface Exposed extends Secret { // TS2320: cannot extend sealed type
 data: string;
}

// Cause 3: Extending incompatible interfaces
interface A {
 x: number;
}
interface B {
 x: string;
}
interface C extends A, B {} // TS2320: incompatible 'x'

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use composition instead of extension

interface MyString {
 value: string;
 toUpperCase(): string;
}

Fix 2: Use type alias for extension

type MyType = Secret & { data: string };

Fix 3: Make properties compatible

interface A {
 x: number;
}
interface B extends A {
 // inherit x from A, don't redefine
}

Related Errors

  • TS2461: Interface incorrectly extends — type conflict.
  • TS2414: Type argument not assignable — generic constraint.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2322 — Type 'X' is not assignable to type 'Y'
item.description:
TS2322 — Type ‘X’ is not assignable to type ‘Y’

TS2322 occurs when you assign a value of one type to a variable, parameter, or return position that expects a different, incompatible type. TypeScript’s structural type system checks that the source type satisfies all requirements of the target type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning a string to a number
let age: number = "twenty-five"; // TS2322

// Cause 2: Missing required properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2322: Property 'age' is missing

// Cause 3: Assigning nullable to non-nullable
let name: string = null; // TS2322 if strictNullChecks is on

// Cause 4: Returning wrong type from function
function getLength(): number {
 return "hello"; // TS2322
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use the correct type

// Wrong
let age: number = "twenty-five";

// Correct
let age: number = 25;

Fix 2: Provide all required properties

const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

Fix 3: Handle nullable values properly

// Accept null
let name: string | null = null;

// Use a default
let name: string = "unknown";

Fix 4: Use type assertions when you know the type

const input = document.getElementById("name") as HTMLInputElement;
input.value = "Alice";

Examples

enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
enum Direction { Up, Down }

let c: Color = Color.Red;
let d: Direction = c; // TS2322

interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000, debug: true }; // TS2322

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — similar but for function arguments.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — specific variant for missing properties.
  • TS2352: Type assertion error — type assertion issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2322 — Type Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2322 — Type ‘X’ is not assignable to type ‘Y’

TS2322 occurs when you assign a value of one type to a variable, parameter, or return position that expects a different, incompatible type. TypeScript’s structural type system checks that the source type satisfies all requirements of the target type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning a string to a number
let age: number = "twenty-five"; // TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'

// Cause 2: Missing required properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2322: Property 'age' is missing

// Cause 3: Assigning nullable to non-nullable
let name: string = null; // TS2322 if strictNullChecks is on

// Cause 4: Returning wrong type from function
function getLength(): number {
 return "hello"; // TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct type

// Wrong
let age: number = "twenty-five";

// Correct
let age: number = 25;

Fix 2: Provide all required properties

// Wrong
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

// Correct
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

Fix 3: Handle nullable values properly

// Wrong
let name: string = null;

// Correct — accept null
let name: string | null = null;

// Correct — use a default
let name: string = "unknown";

Fix 4: Use type assertions when you know the type

const input = document.getElementById("name") as HTMLInputElement;
input.value = "Alice";

Examples

// Example 1: Enum mismatch
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
let c: Color = Color.Red;

enum Direction { Up, Down }
let d: Direction = c; // TS2322: Type 'Color' is not assignable to type 'Direction'

// Example 2: Excess properties in object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000, debug: true }; // TS2322

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable — similar but for function arguments.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — specific variant for missing properties.
  • TS2352: Conversion may be a mistake — type assertion issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2322 v2 — Type Not Assignable to Type Fix
item.description:
TS2322 — Type ‘X’ is not assignable to type ‘Y’ (v2)

This variant of TS2322 covers complex assignment errors involving generics, union types, conditional types, and library-provided types where the type mismatch is less obvious.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'string[]'
  • TS2322: Type 'number | undefined' is not assignable to type 'number'
  • TS2322: Type 'Element' is not assignable to type 'ReactElement'
  • TS2322: Type 'Dispatch<SetStateAction<T>>' is not assignable to type '...

These often appear when working with React hooks, third-party library types, or when strict null checks expose hidden type mismatches.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 — Property 'X' does not exist on type 'Y'
item.description:
TS2339 — Property ‘X’ does not exist on type ‘Y’

TS2339 occurs when you try to access a property on a type that does not define it. TypeScript’s type system enforces that you can only access properties that exist on the declared type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing non-existent property
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.email); // TS2339

// Cause 2: Wrong type annotation
const data: string = "hello";
data.length; // OK
data.toFixed(2); // TS2339: string has no toFixed

// Cause 3: Using API response without type definition
const response = fetch("/api/user");
const data = await response.json();
console.log(data.user.name); // TS2339: 'user' does not exist

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add missing property to interface

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

console.log(user?.email);

Fix 3: Type the API response

interface ApiResponse {
 user: { name: string; age: number };
}
const data: ApiResponse = await response.json();
console.log(data.user.name);

Fix 4: Use type assertion or index signature

interface StringMap {
 [key: string]: string;
}
const map: StringMap = { name: "Alice" };
console.log(map["name"]);

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property is type-only import — type-only import access.
  • TS2551: Property does not exist - did you mean — typo suggestions.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required property.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 — Property 'X' is a type-only import
item.description:
TS2339 — Property ‘X’ is a type-only import

TS2339 can occur when you try to use a type-only import as a value. With verbatimModuleSyntax or isolatedModules, TypeScript enforces that type-only imports cannot be used at runtime.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using type import as value
import type { User } from "./types";
const u: User = { name: "Alice" }; // OK as type annotation
const u2 = new User(); // TS2339: User is type-only

// Cause 2: Re-exporting type as value
import type { Config } from "./config";
export { Config }; // TS2339: Config is type-only

// Cause 3: Import without 'type' used as type-only
import { Logger } from "./logger";
// Logger is only used as type, but imported as value

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use import type correctly

import type { User } from "./types";
// Can only use User as a type
const u: User = { name: "Alice" };

Fix 2: Separate value and type imports

import { helper } from "./utils";
import type { Config } from "./config";

Fix 3: Use inline type import

import { helper, type Config } from "./module";

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — regular property access.
  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — type used as value.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 — Property Does Not Exist on Type
item.description:
TS2339 — Property ‘X’ does not exist on type ‘Y’

TS2339 occurs when you try to access a property on a type that doesn’t declare that property. TypeScript enforces property access based on the declared shape of types, so accessing a non-existent property is a compile-time error.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing a property not in the interface
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.email); // TS2339: Property 'email' does not exist on type 'User'

// Cause 2: Typo in property name
console.log(user.nme); // TS2339: Did you mean 'name'?

// Cause 3: Using 'any' bypass but the real type is stricter
const response: any = fetch("/api");
console.log(response.data.id); // Compiles with any, fails at runtime

// Cause 4: Accessing array methods on non-array
const num: number = 42;
num.map(x => x); // TS2339: Property 'map' does not exist on type 'number'

Solutions

Fix 1: Add the property to the type definition

// Wrong
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

// Correct — add email to the interface
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}

Fix 2: Use optional properties or index signatures

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email?: string; // Optional
}

// Or allow any extra properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 [key: string]: unknown;
}

Fix 3: Fix typos in property names

// Wrong
console.log(user.nme);

// Correct
console.log(user.name);

Fix 4: Use type guards before accessing properties

function processUser(user: User | Admin) {
 if ("email" in user) {
 console.log(user.email); // OK — TypeScript knows it's Admin
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: API response typed too narrowly
interface ApiResponse {
 data: string;
}
const res: ApiResponse = { data: "hello" };
console.log(res.data.length); // TS2339 if data could be undefined

// Example 2: Class without a property
class Animal {
 name: string = "";
}
const cat: Animal = new Animal();
cat.sound = "meow"; // TS2339: Property 'sound' does not exist on type 'Animal'

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope at all.
  • TS2345: Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable — type mismatch in function calls.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties on object literal.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 v2 — Property Does Not Exist on Type Fix
item.description:
TS2339 — Property Does Not Exist on Type (v2)

This variant of TS2339 covers situations where property access fails on union types, generic constraints, or when working with third-party library types that lack proper typings.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2339: Property 'name' does not exist on type 'User | null'
  • TS2339: Property 'data' does not exist on type 'unknown'
  • TS2339: Property 'map' does not exist on type 'T'
  • TS2339: Property 'toISOString' does not exist on type 'string | Date'

TypeScript prevents accessing properties that the current type annotation doesn’t include. This often happens after narrowing, with union types, or when the type is inferred as unknown.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2344 — Type 'X' does not satisfy constraint 'Y'
item.description:
TS2344 — Type ‘X’ does not satisfy constraint ‘Y’

TS2344 occurs when a type argument passed to a generic does not satisfy the declared extends constraint.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Type doesn't satisfy constraint
interface HasLength {
 length: number;
}

function logLength<T extends HasLength>(arg: T) {
 console.log(arg.length);
}
logLength(42); // TS2344: 'number' does not satisfy 'HasLength'

// Cause 2: Wrong type argument
class Container<T extends string> {}
const c: Container<number> = new Container(); // TS2344

// Cause 3: Partial constraint satisfaction
interface Serializable {
 serialize(): string;
}
class MyClass {
 name = "test";
}
function serialize<T extends Serializable>(obj: T) {}
serialize(new MyClass()); // TS2344: MyClass missing serialize()

How to Fix

Fix 1: Pass a satisfying type

logLength("hello"); // string has length
logLength([1, 2]); // array has length

Fix 2: Implement the constraint

class MyClass implements Serializable {
 name = "test";
 serialize() { return this.name; }
}

Fix 3: Relax the constraint

function logLength<T>(arg: T) {
 if (typeof arg === "string" || Array.isArray(arg)) {
 console.log(arg.length);
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2414: Type argument not assignable — similar generic constraint.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — interface constraint.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2345 — Argument of type 'X' is not assignable to parameter
item.description:
TS2345 — Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable to parameter of type ‘Y’

TS2345 occurs when you pass an argument to a function that does not match the expected parameter type. This is one of the most common TypeScript errors and usually indicates a type mismatch at a call site.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Passing wrong type to function
function greet(name: string) {
 console.log(`Hello, ${name}`);
}
greet(42); // TS2345: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'

// Cause 2: Nullable argument to non-nullable parameter
function process(value: number) {
 return value * 2;
}
process(null); // TS2345 if strictNullChecks is on

// Cause 3: Array type mismatch
function logItems(items: string[]) {
 items.forEach(item => console.log(item));
}
logItems([1, 2, 3]); // TS2345

How to Fix

Fix 1: Pass the correct type

greet("Alice");

Fix 2: Handle null with union type

function process(value: number | null) {
 if (value === null) return 0;
 return value * 2;
}

Fix 3: Map array to correct type

logItems([1, 2, 3].map(String));

Fix 4: Use overloads for flexible signatures

function format(input: string): string;
function format(input: number): string;
function format(input: string | number): string {
 return String(input);
}
format("hello");
format(42);

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — assignment rather than call site.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — wrong argument count.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — no overload accepts the arguments.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2345 — Argument Type Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2345 — Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable

TS2345 occurs when you pass an argument to a function that doesn’t match the expected parameter type. This is one of the most common TypeScript errors and covers a wide range of type incompatibilities.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Wrong primitive type
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42); // TS2345: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'

// Cause 2: Missing required properties in object argument
function createUser(user: { name: string; age: number }) {}
createUser({ name: "Alice" }); // TS2345: Property 'age' is missing

// Cause 3: Array type mismatch
function processItems(items: number[]) {}
processItems(["a", "b"]); // TS2345: Type 'string[]' not assignable to 'number[]'

// Cause 4: Nullable argument to non-nullable parameter
function handle(input: string) {}
handle(null); // TS2345 if strictNullChecks is on

Solutions

Fix 1: Match the expected argument type

// Wrong
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42);

// Correct
greet("Alice");

Fix 2: Provide all required properties

// Wrong
function createUser(user: { name: string; age: number }) {}
createUser({ name: "Alice" });

// Correct
createUser({ name: "Alice", age: 30 });

Fix 3: Cast or convert arguments to the expected type

function processItems(items: number[]) {}
processItems(["1", "2"].map(Number)); // Convert strings to numbers

Fix 4: Use union types to accept multiple types

function handle(input: string | null) {
 if (input === null) return;
 // input is string here
}
handle(null); // OK

Examples

// Example 1: Incompatible object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
function startServer(config: Config) {}
startServer({ host: "localhost" }); // TS2345

// Example 2: Array vs single value
function log(items: string[]) {}
log("hello"); // TS2345: 'string' is not assignable to 'string[]'

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — assignment context rather than argument context.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — function overloads don’t match any signature.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments, but got Y — wrong argument count.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2345 v2 — Argument Type Mismatch Fix
item.description:
TS2345 — Argument Type Mismatch (v2)

This variant of TS2345 covers complex argument type mismatches including callback parameters, generic inference failures, and situations where the expected type is a complex mapped or conditional type.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2345: Argument of type 'string' is not assignable to parameter of type 'number'
  • TS2345: Argument of type '(x: number) => string' is not assignable to parameter of type '(x: number) => number'
  • TS2345: Argument of type 'User' is not assignable to parameter of type 'Partial<User>'
  • TS2345: Type '0' is not assignable to type '1'

This error fires when you call a function with arguments whose types don’t match the parameter types in the function signature.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2349 — This expression is not callable
item.description:
TS2349 — This expression is not callable
