TS2349 occurs when you try to call an expression as a function, but its type does not include a call signature. The expression is not a function or callable object.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling a non-function variable
const name: string = "Alice";
name(); // TS2349: This expression is not callable

// Cause 2: Importing a module but calling the wrong thing
import config from "./config";
config(); // TS230 if config is an object, not a function

// Cause 3: Calling a class as a function
class Foo {}
Foo(); // TS2349 — must use 'new'

// Cause 4: Overloaded function not callable as expected
function create(): string;
function create(n: number): number[];
function create(n?: number): string | number[] {
 return n ? [] : "";
}
create; // TS2349 if accessed without calling

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify the variable is a function

const greet = (name: string) => `Hello, ${name}`;
greet("Alice"); // OK

Fix 2: Use ’new’ for class instantiation

const instance = new Foo();

Fix 3: Check import name

import { createConfig } from "./config";
createConfig(); // call the actual function

Related Errors

  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — calling a type as a value.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — argument count mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2349 — This Expression Is Not Callable
item.description:
TS2349 — This expression is not callable

TS2349 occurs when you try to call a value as a function using (), but its type doesn’t have a call signature. This happens when a variable holds a non-function value or when a type is not callable.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling a non-function value
const name = "Alice";
name(); // TS2349: This expression is not callable

// Cause 2: Calling an object as a function
const obj = { greet: "hello" };
obj(); // TS2349

// Cause 3: Calling a class instance as a function
class Greeter {
 greet() { return "hello"; }
}
const g = new Greeter();
g(); // TS2349

// Cause 4: Property that resolves to a non-function
interface Service {
 handler: string | (() => void);
}
function invoke(service: Service) {
 service.handler(); // TS2349: handler might be a string
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Verify the value is actually a function

// Wrong
const name = "Alice";
name();

// Correct
const greet = () => "Hello, Alice!";
greet();

Fix 2: Use a type guard before calling

interface Service {
 handler: string | (() => void);
}

function invoke(service: Service) {
 if (typeof service.handler === "function") {
 service.handler(); // OK
 }
}

Fix 3: Call the correct method

// Wrong
const obj = { greet: "hello" };
obj();

// Correct
const obj = { greet: () => "hello" };
obj.greet();

Fix 4: Ensure proper function assignment

// Wrong — property is not a function
const config = {
 init: false,
};
config.init(); // TS2349

// Correct
const config = {
 init: () => console.log("initialized"),
};
config.init();

Examples

// Example 1: Module default export is not a function
import myModule from "./my-module";
myModule(); // TS2349 if default export is an object

// Example 2: Array methods confused with call
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr(); // TS2349: arrays are not callable

Related Errors

  • TS2351: This expression is not constructable — trying to use new on non-constructable.
  • TS2688: Type has no matching call signature — callable but wrong arguments.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — callable but wrong argument types.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2351 — This Expression Is Not Constructable
item.description:
TS2351 — This expression is not constructable

TS2351 occurs when you try to use new on a value whose type doesn’t have a construct signature. This happens when calling new on a function that isn’t a constructor, an object, or a primitive.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using 'new' on a regular function
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
const g = new greet("Alice"); // TS2351: not constructable

// Cause 2: Using 'new' on an arrow function
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create(); // TS2351

// Cause 3: Using 'new' on a module default export that isn't a class
import utils from "./utils";
const u = new utils(); // TS2351

// Cause 4: Using 'new' on an interface
interface Factory {
 create(): Widget;
}
const f = new Factory(); // TS2351: interfaces aren't values

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a class with a constructor

// Wrong — regular function
function Greeter(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // TS2351

// Correct — use a class
class Greeter {
 name: string;
 constructor(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
 }
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // OK

Fix 2: Use factory function without ’new'

// Wrong
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create();

// Correct — call without 'new'
const obj = create();

Fix 3: Import the class correctly

// Wrong — importing non-class default export
import utils from "./utils";
new utils();

// Correct — import a class
import { MyClass } from "./my-class";
new MyClass();

Fix 4: Define a construct signature for the type

interface Constructor {
 new (name: string): { name: string };
}

function createInstance(Ctor: Constructor, name: string) {
 return new Ctor(name); // OK
}

Examples

// Example 1: Using 'new' on a function stored in a variable
const initialize = function () {};
const app = new initialize(); // TS2351

// Example 2: Using 'new' on a namespace
namespace Utils {
 export function helper() {}
}
new Utils(); // TS2351

Related Errors

  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — trying to call () on non-callable.
  • TS2688: Type has no matching call signature — callable but wrong arguments.
  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — property access issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2352 — Conversion May Be a Mistake
item.description:
TS2352 — Conversion of type ‘X’ to type ‘Y’ may be a mistake

TS2352 occurs when you use a type assertion (as or angle brackets) to convert between two unrelated types. TypeScript warns that the assertion might be incorrect because the types don’t overlap structurally. This is stricter in --strict mode.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Asserting between unrelated types
const value: string = "hello";
const num: number = value as number; // TS2352: Conversion may be a mistake

// Cause 2: Asserting object literals to incompatible types
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const data = { x: 1, y: 2 };
const user = data as User; // TS2352: types don't overlap

// Cause 3: Using angle bracket assertion
const value = "hello";
const num = <number>value; // TS2352

// Cause 4: Assertion between primitive types
let a: boolean = true;
let b: number = a as number; // TS2352

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a more appropriate assertion

// Wrong
const value: string = "123";
const num: number = value as number; // TS2352

// Correct — use actual conversion
const num: number = Number(value);
const num2: number = parseInt(value, 10);

Fix 2: Use a type guard instead of assertion

function isUser(obj: unknown): obj is User {
 return (
 typeof obj === "object" &&
 obj !== null &&
 "name" in obj &&
 "age" in obj
 );
}

const data = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
if (isUser(data)) {
 // data is User
}

Fix 3: Use unknown as intermediate type

// Wrong
const value: string = "hello";
const num: number = value as number;

// Correct — go through unknown
const intermediate: unknown = value;
// Then narrow or convert properly

Fix 4: Ensure structural compatibility

// If types need to be compatible, ensure they share structure
interface Source {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
interface Target {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const source: Source = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
const target: Target = source as Target; // OK — structural match

Examples

// Example 1: API response typed incorrectly
interface ApiResponse {
 data: { id: number; name: string };
}
const response = await fetch("/api");
const json = await response.json();
const result = json as ApiResponse; // TS2352 — json is 'any'

// Example 2: DOM element type mismatch
const div = document.getElementById("app");
const canvas = div as HTMLCanvasElement; // TS2352 if strict

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — direct assignment without assertion.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — function argument issues.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — needs type narrowing.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2356 — Type 'X' has no properties in common with type 'Y'
item.description:
TS2356 — Type ‘X’ has no properties in common with type ‘Y’

TS2356 occurs when you compare or assign two types that share no common properties. TypeScript’s structural type system finds zero overlap between the types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Comparing unrelated types
interface Cat {
 purr(): void;
}
interface Car {
 drive(): void;
}

const cat: Cat = { purr() {} };
const car: Car = { drive() {} };

if (cat === car) { // TS2356
 console.log("same");
}

// Cause 2: Assigning incompatible types
const cat2: Cat = car; // TS2356

// Cause 3: Union with no overlap
type Result = string & number; // TS2356

How to Fix

Fix 1: Don’t compare incompatible types

// Remove the comparison or use a common type
interface Pet {
 name: string;
}
interface Cat extends Pet { purr(): void; }
interface Dog extends Pet { bark(): void; }

Fix 2: Use type guard or discriminator

function isCat(val: Cat | Car): val is Cat {
 return "purr" in val;
}

Fix 3: Fix the union type

type Result = string | number; // use union, not intersection

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.
  • TS2367: Comparison unintentional — impossible comparison.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2362 — The left-hand side of arithmetic must be
item.description:
TS2362 — The left-hand side of arithmetic must be a ’number’, ‘bigint’, or ‘any’ type

TS2362 occurs when you use arithmetic operators on a value that is not a numeric type. The left-hand operand must be number, bigint, or any.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Arithmetic on string
const result = "5" + 3; // TS2362 or implicit conversion

// Cause 2: Arithmetic on boolean
const val = true + 1; // TS2362

// Cause 3: Arithmetic on object
const obj = { value: 5 };
const sum = obj + 1; // TS2362

How to Fix

Fix 1: Convert to number

const result = Number("5") + 3;
// or
const result = parseInt("5") + 3;

Fix 2: Use proper types

const val = true ? 1 : 0;
const sum = val + 1;

Fix 3: Extract numeric value

const obj = { value: 5 };
const sum = obj.value + 1;

Related Errors

  • TS2363: Right-hand side of arithmetic must be — right-hand side variant.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2363 — The right-hand side of arithmetic must be
item.description:
TS2363 — The right-hand side of arithmetic must be a ’number’, ‘bigint’, or ‘any’ type

TS2363 occurs when the right-hand operand of an arithmetic operation is not a numeric type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: String on right-hand side
const result = 5 + "hello"; // TS2363

// Cause 2: Boolean on right-hand side
const val = 1 + true; // TS2363

// Cause 3: Object on right-hand side
const obj = { value: 3 };
const sum = 10 + obj; // TS2363

How to Fix

Fix 1: Convert to number

const result = 5 + Number("3");

Fix 2: Use proper types

const val = 1 + (true ? 1 : 0);

Fix 3: Extract numeric value

const sum = 10 + obj.value;

Related Errors

  • TS2362: Left-hand side of arithmetic must be — left-hand side variant.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2367 — Comparison Appears to Be Unintentional
item.description:
TS2367 — This comparison appears to be unintentional

TS2367 occurs when a comparison is always true or always false because the two sides of the comparison have types with no overlap. TypeScript flags these as likely bugs — you’re comparing values that can never be equal (or always are).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Comparing a type with a value it can never hold
let status: "active" | "inactive";
if (status === "deleted") { // TS2367: This comparison appears to be unintentional
 // "deleted" is not in the union
}

// Cause 2: Comparing a string literal union with an invalid value
type Color = "red" | "green" | "blue";
const isRed = color === "yellow"; // TS2367

// Cause 3: Comparing a number enum with a non-existent member
enum Direction { Up, Down }
const isLeft = Direction.Up === 2; // TS2367: Comparison always false

// Cause 4: Comparing 'never' type with anything
function exhaustive(x: never) {
 if (x === "something") {} // TS2367
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Use valid values from the union

// Wrong
let status: "active" | "inactive";
if (status === "deleted") {} // TS2367

// Correct
if (status === "active") {} // Valid comparison

Fix 2: Widen the type to accept the comparison value

// Wrong
let status: "active" | "inactive";

// Correct — if "deleted" is a valid state, add it to the union
let status: "active" | "inactive" | "deleted";
if (status === "deleted") {} // OK

Fix 3: Remove impossible branches

type Fruit = "apple" | "banana";

function describe(fruit: Fruit) {
 if (fruit === "orange") {
 // This branch is unreachable — TS2367
 return "This never happens";
 }
 return fruit; // Just handle valid cases
}

Fix 4: Use a type guard function for runtime checks

function isString(value: unknown): value is string {
 return typeof value === "string";
}

let x: number | string;
if (isString(x)) {
 // x is string here
}

Examples

// Example 1: Exhaustive switch without proper typing
type Shape = "circle" | "square";
function area(shape: Shape) {
 switch (shape) {
 case "circle": return Math.PI;
 case "square": return 1;
 case "triangle": return 0; // TS2367
 }
}

// Example 2: Comparing different enum types
enum A { X, Y }
enum B { X, Y }
const eq = A.X === B.X; // TS2367: A.X and B.X are different types

Related Errors

  • TS2366: A function whose declared type is neither ‘undefined’ nor ‘void’ must return a value — related to unreachable code.
  • TS2345: Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable — passing invalid values to functions.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — untyped values need narrowing.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2367 — This comparison appears to be unintentional
item.description:
TS2367 — This comparison appears to be unintentional

TS2367 occurs when you compare two values that TypeScript knows can never be equal due to their types. This is a type safety check to prevent logical errors.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Comparing unrelated types
let x: string = "hello";
if (x === 42) { // TS2367: this comparison is always false
 console.log("match");
}

// Cause 2: Comparing enum values
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
enum Size { Small, Medium, Large }

let c: Color = Color.Red;
if (c === Size.Small) { // TS2367
 console.log("match");
}

// Cause 3: Comparing with wrong type literal
let status: "active" | "inactive";
if (status === "deleted") { // TS2367
 console.log("deleted");
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Compare compatible types

let x: string = "hello";
if (x === "world") { // OK
 console.log("match");
}

Fix 2: Cast if comparison is intentional

if (x as any === 42) {
 console.log("match");
}

Fix 3: Use proper type narrowing

let status: "active" | "inactive";
if (status === "active") { // OK
 console.log("active");
}

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — type mismatch in assignment.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — type mismatch in call.
  • TS2362: Left-hand side of arithmetic must be — arithmetic type errors.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2367 v2 — Comparison Appears Unintentional Fix
item.description:
TS2367 — Comparison Appears Unintentional (v2)

This variant of TS2367 covers comparisons between types that are provably unequal, including literal type comparisons, boolean checks against non-boolean values, and exhaustive switch statements.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2367: This comparison appears to be unintentional because the types '"admin"' and '"user"' have no overlap.
  • TS2367: This comparison appears to be unintentional because the types 'boolean' and 'number' have no overlap.
  • TS2367: This comparison appears to be unintentional because the types 'string' and 'number' have no overlap.

TypeScript detects when a comparison can never be true based on the types of the operands. This often means the comparison is redundant or there’s a bug in the logic.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2391 — Function with qualified name
item.description:
TS2391 — Function with qualified name

TS2391 occurs when you try to redeclare or reinitialize a function that already has a qualified name in the current scope. This typically happens with duplicate function declarations or incorrect namespace usage.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Duplicate function declaration with different signature
function greet(name: string): void;
function greet(name: string, greeting: string): void;
function greet(name: string, greeting?: string): void {
 console.log(greeting || "Hello", name);
}
// Redeclaring causes TS2391
function greet(name: string) {} // TS2391

// Cause 2: Trying to reassign a function in namespace
namespace Utils {
 export function helper() {}
}
Utils.helper = function() {}; // TS2391

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use function overloads properly

function greet(name: string): void;
function greet(name: string, greeting: string): void;
function greet(name: string, greeting?: string): void {
 console.log(greeting || "Hello", name);
}

Fix 2: Use const for function reassignment

let greet = (name: string) => `Hello, ${name}`;
greet = (name: string) => `Hi, ${name}`; // OK with let

Fix 3: Avoid duplicate declarations

// Only one declaration per scope
function helper() {}

Related Errors

  • TS2300: Duplicate identifier — duplicate names.
  • TS2391: Function redeclaration — function already declared.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2412 — Property accessors can only be used with qualified names
item.description:
TS2412 — Property accessors can only be used with qualified names

TS2412 occurs when you use a property accessor on an expression that is not a qualified name (i.e., not a namespace or module reference).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Wrong property accessor syntax
type Foo = { bar: string };
const foo: Foo = { bar: "hello" };
Foo.bar; // TS2412: cannot use accessor on type

// Cause 2: Accessing static member incorrectly
class MyClass {
 static value = 42;
}
MyClass.value; // OK

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use instance access

const foo: Foo = { bar: "hello" };
foo.bar; // OK

Fix 2: Use correct static access

MyClass.value; // correct for static

Fix 3: Use namespace access correctly

namespace MyNamespace {
 export const value = 42;
}
MyNamespace.value; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist — property access errors.
  • TS2571: Object is of type ‘unknown’ — unknown type access.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2414 — Type argument not assignable
item.description:
TS2414 — Type argument not assignable

TS2414 occurs when a type argument passed to a generic type or function does not satisfy the declared constraints.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Type doesn't satisfy constraint
interface HasLength {
 length: number;
}

function logLength<T extends HasLength>(arg: T) {
 console.log(arg.length);
}
logLength(42); // TS2414: 'number' does not satisfy 'HasLength'

// Cause 2: Wrong type argument to generic class
class Container<T extends string> {
 value: T;
}
const c: Container<number> = { value: 42 }; // TS2414

// Cause 3: Interface extending with wrong type
interface StringMap<T extends string> {
 [key: string]: T;
}
const m: StringMap<number> = {}; // TS2414

How to Fix

Fix 1: Pass a type that satisfies the constraint

logLength("hello"); // string has length
logLength([1, 2, 3]); // array has length

Fix 2: Relax or change the constraint

class Container<T> {
 value: T;
}
const c: Container<number> = { value: 42 }; // OK

Fix 3: Ensure interface extension is valid

interface NumberMap<T extends number> {
 [key: string]: T;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2344: Type does not satisfy constraint — constraint failure.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — interface implementation.
  • TS2461: Interface incorrectly extends — interface extension.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2420 — Class incorrectly implements interface
item.description:
TS2420 — Class incorrectly implements interface

TS2420 occurs when a class that implements an interface does not provide all required members or provides members with incompatible types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing required methods
interface Logger {
 log(message: string): void;
 error(message: string): void;
}

class ConsoleLogger implements Logger {
 log(message: string) {
 console.log(message);
 }
 // missing error() — TS2420
}

// Cause 2: Wrong property types
interface Config {
 port: number;
}

class AppConfig implements Config {
 port: string; // TS2420: 'string' is not assignable to 'number'
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Implement all required members

class ConsoleLogger implements Logger {
 log(message: string) {
 console.log(message);
 }
 error(message: string) {
 console.error(message);
 }
}

Fix 2: Use the correct types

class AppConfig implements Config {
 port: number = 3000;
}

Fix 3: Use abstract class for partial implementation

abstract class BaseLogger implements Logger {
 abstract log(message: string): void;
 abstract error(message: string): void;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Class is not abstract and does not implement — abstract implementation.
  • TS2717: Non-abstract class does not implement — similar variant.
  • TS2414: Type argument not assignable — generic constraint issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2454 — Variable 'X' is used before being assigned
item.description:
TS2454 — Variable ‘X’ is used before being assigned

TS2454 occurs when strictPropertyInitialization or strictNullChecks detects that a variable may be used before it has been assigned a value.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Variable declared but not initialized before use
let name: string;
console.log(name); // TS2454: used before assigned

// Cause 2: Class property not initialized
class User {
 name: string; // TS2454 if not initialized in constructor
 constructor() {
 // forgot to assign name
 }
}

// Cause 3: Conditional assignment not guaranteed
let value: number;
if (Math.random() > 0.5) {
 value = 1;
}
console.log(value); // TS2454: value may not be assigned

How to Fix

Fix 1: Initialize at declaration

let name: string = "Alice";

Fix 2: Initialize in constructor or use definite assignment assertion

class User {
 name!: string; // definite assignment assertion
 constructor() {
 this.name = "Alice";
 }
}

Fix 3: Use a default value

let value: number = 0;
if (Math.random() > 0.5) {
 value = 1;
}

Fix 4: Use definite assignment assertion (non-null)

class Component {
 el!: HTMLElement; // tells TS "I'll initialize this"
 init() {
 this.el = document.getElementById("app")!;
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — undefined access.
  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — null access.
  • TS2722: Cannot invoke object which is possibly ‘callable’ — undefined function call.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2459 — Type 'X' has no property 'Y'
item.description:
TS2459 — Type ‘X’ has no property ‘Y’ and no string index signature

TS2459 occurs when you try to access a property on a type that doesn’t have it and doesn’t have an index signature that would allow arbitrary property access.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing property on wrong type
type StringOrNumber = string | number;
const val: StringOrNumber = "hello";
val.toFixed(2); // TS2459: 'string | number' has no property 'toFixed'

// Cause 2: Missing index signature
interface User {
 name: string;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };
user["email"] = "test"; // TS2459: no string index signature

// Cause 3: Union type property access
type A = { x: number };
type B = { y: number };
const val: A | B = { x: 1 };
val.x; // TS2459: 'y' is not common

How to Fix

Fix 1: Narrow the type first

if (typeof val === "number") {
 val.toFixed(2); // OK
}

Fix 2: Add index signature

interface User {
 name: string;
 [key: string]: string;
}

Fix 3: Use discriminated union

type A = { kind: "a"; x: number };
type B = { kind: "b"; y: number };
function handle(val: A | B) {
 if (val.kind === "a") {
 console.log(val.x);
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist — property access errors.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — type mismatch in call.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — assignment type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2461 — Interface 'X' incorrectly extends 'Y'
item.description:
TS2461 — Interface ‘X’ incorrectly extends ‘Y’

TS2461 occurs when an interface that extends another interface has incompatible types for inherited properties.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Conflicting property types
interface Animal {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

interface Robot extends Animal {
 age: string; // TS2461: 'string' is not assignable to 'number'
}

// Cause 2: Incompatible method signatures
interface Logger {
 log(msg: string): void;
}

interface StrictLogger extends Logger {
 log(msg: number): void; // TS2461: incompatible signature
}

// Cause 3: Incompatible with built-in type
interface MyArray extends Array<string> {
 push(n: number): void; // TS2461
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use compatible types

interface Robot extends Animal {
 age: number; // same type as Animal.age
 model: string;
}

Fix 2: Use same method signature

interface StrictLogger extends Logger {
 log(msg: string): void; // compatible
}

Fix 3: Use composition instead

interface Logger {
 log(msg: string): void;
}

interface StrictLogger {
 log(msg: string, strict: boolean): void;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2320: Interface cannot extend — interface extension failure.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — class interface mismatch.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2464 — Circular definition of import alias
item.description:
TS2464 — Circular definition of import alias

TS2464 occurs when an import alias refers back to itself through a chain of imports, creating a circular reference. This makes it impossible for TypeScript to resolve the type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Direct circular import
// file: a.ts
import { B } from "./b";
export type A = B;

// file: b.ts
import { A } from "./a";
export type B = A; // TS2464: circular definition

// Cause 2: Indirect circular import
// file: a.ts
import { C } from "./c";
export type A = C;

// file: b.ts
import { A } from "./a";
export type B = A;

// file: c.ts
import { B } from "./b";
export type C = B; // circular

How to Fix

Fix 1: Break the cycle with a shared type file

// file: types.ts
export interface User {
 name: string;
}

// file: a.ts
import { User } from "./types";
export type A = User;

// file: b.ts
import { User } from "./types";
export type B = User;

Fix 2: Use type-only imports

import type { B } from "./b"; // type-only, no runtime circular

Fix 3: Restructure to remove the cycle

// Move shared types to a common module
// file: shared.ts
export type SharedType = { /* ... */ };

// files import from shared.ts instead of each other

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — broken imports.
  • TS2300: Duplicate identifier — naming conflicts.
  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — type-only usage.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2464 — Class Can Only Implement an Identifier
item.description:
TS2464 — A class can only implement an identifier/qualified-name

TS2464 occurs when you use implements with an expression that isn’t a valid identifier or qualified name. You can only implement named interfaces or classes, not object literals, unions, or computed values.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Implementing an object literal
class MyClass implements { name: string; age: number } { // TS2464
 name = "";
 age = 0;
}

// Cause 2: Implementing a union type
interface A { a: string; }
interface B { b: string; }
class MyClass implements A | B { // TS2464
 a = "";
}

// Cause 3: Implementing an expression
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {} // TS2464

// Cause 4: Implementing an inline type without a name
class MyClass implements { id: number } {} // TS2464

Solutions

Fix 1: Define an interface and implement it

// Wrong
class MyClass implements { name: string } {}

// Correct
interface Named {
 name: string;
}
class MyClass implements Named {
 name = "";
}

Fix 2: Only implement one interface (not a union)

// Wrong
class MyClass implements A | B {}

// Correct — implement both separately
class MyClass implements A, B {
 a = "";
 b = "";
}

Fix 3: Use interface reference instead of inline type

// Wrong
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {}

// Correct
interface HasX {
 x: number;
}
class MyClass implements HasX {
 x = 0;
}

Fix 4: Implement named type aliases

type Printable = { print(): void };
class MyClass implements Printable {
 print() { console.log("printed"); }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Trying to implement a generic type without instantiation
interface Container<T> { value: T; }
class MyClass implements Container { // TS2464 — missing type argument
 value = "";
}

// Fix:
class MyClass implements Container<string> {
 value = "";
}

// Example 2: Implementing a mapped type
type Options = { [key: string]: string };
class Config implements Options {} // TS2464 — can't implement mapped type directly

Related Errors

  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements interface — implements but missing members.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2469 — X cannot be used as a namespace
item.description:
TS2469 — ‘X’ cannot be used as a namespace

TS2469 occurs when you try to use a module or class as a namespace. Only namespace declarations can be used with dot notation for sub-members.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using module as namespace
import React from "react";
React.createElement("div"); // OK if React has this export

// Cause 2: Trying to access sub-members of class
class MyClass {
 static value = 42;
}
MyClass.Nested; // TS2469: MyClass cannot be used as namespace

// Cause 3: Wrong namespace access
import * as utils from "./utils";
utils.sub.value; // TS2469 if 'sub' is not a namespace

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use proper import/require

import { something } from "./module";
// or
const { something } = require("./module");

Fix 2: Use static members correctly

MyClass.value; // access static property directly

Fix 3: Create a proper namespace

namespace Utils {
 export const sub = {
 value: 42,
 };
}
Utils.sub.value; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2503: Cannot find namespace — namespace not found.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2503 — Cannot find namespace 'X'
item.description:
TS2503 — Cannot find namespace ‘X’

TS2503 occurs when you reference a namespace that TypeScript cannot find in the current scope. This can happen if the namespace is not declared, imported, or is in a different file without proper reference.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Referencing undeclared namespace
const val: MyNamespace.Type = {}; // TS2503: Cannot find namespace 'MyNamespace'

// Cause 2: Missing triple-slash reference
// file: types.d.ts declares namespace GlobalTypes
// file: app.ts uses GlobalTypes without reference

// Cause 3: Wrong namespace import
namespace A {
 export type Item = { id: number };
}
const item: B.Item = { id: 1 }; // TS2503

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add triple-slash reference

/// <reference path="./types.d.ts" />
const val: MyNamespace.Type = {};

Fix 2: Import the namespace

import { MyNamespace } from "./types";
const val: MyNamespace.Type = {};

Fix 3: Fix the namespace name

const item: A.Item = { id: 1 };

Fix 4: Use ambient declaration

declare namespace MyNamespace {
 type Type = { /* ... */ };
}

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2694: Namespace has no exported member — member not found.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2531 — Index signature in type 'X' requires property 'Y'
item.description:
TS2531 — Index signature in type ‘X’ requires property ‘Y’

TS2531 can occur when an index signature requires all properties of the type to match the index signature’s value type, but a specific property doesn’t comply.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Property type doesn't match index signature
interface StringMap {
 [key: string]: string;
 count: number; // TS2531: 'number' is not assignable to 'string'
}

// Cause 2: Mixed types with index signature
interface Mixed {
 name: string;
 [key: string]: string | number;
 age: boolean; // TS2531: 'boolean' not in union
}

// Cause 3: Property incompatible with index
interface Dict {
 [key: string]: string;
 length: number; // TS2531
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Make property match index signature

interface StringMap {
 [key: string]: string;
 count: string; // now matches
}

Fix 2: Widen index signature type

interface StringMap {
 [key: string]: string | number;
 count: number;
}

Fix 3: Remove index signature

interface Config {
 name: string;
 count: number;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist — property access errors.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.
  • TS2459: Type has no property — missing property.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2531 — Object is possibly 'null'
item.description:
TS2531 — Object is possibly ’null'

TS2531 occurs when you access a property or call a method on a value that TypeScript knows might be null. This is enforced when strictNullChecks is enabled.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: getElementById can return null
const el = document.getElementById("myId");
el.style.color = "red"; // TS2531: Object is possibly 'null'

// Cause 2: Array.find can return undefined
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
const found = arr.find(x => x > 5);
found.toFixed(); // TS2531: Object is possibly 'undefined'

// Cause 3: Nullable function parameter
function process(el: HTMLElement | null) {
 el.innerHTML = "hello"; // TS2531
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use non-null assertion (when you’re sure)

const el = document.getElementById("myId")!;
el.style.color = "red";

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

el?.style.color = "red";

Fix 3: Add null check

const el = document.getElementById("myId");
if (el) {
 el.style.color = "red";
}

Fix 4: Use a guard clause

function process(el: HTMLElement | null) {
 if (!el) return;
 el.innerHTML = "hello";
}

Related Errors

  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — undefined variant.
  • TS18047: X is possibly ’null’ — variable-level null check.
  • TS18048: X is possibly ‘undefined’ — variable-level undefined check.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2531 — Object Is Possibly 'null'
item.description:
TS2531 — Object is possibly ’null'

TS2531 occurs when you access a property or call a method on an object that could be null. This is enforced when strictNullChecks is enabled in your tsconfig, which is the default in modern TypeScript projects.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: DOM query that might return null
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello"; // TS2531: Object is possibly 'null'

// Cause 2: Function parameter that accepts null
function process(value: string | null) {
 console.log(value.length); // TS2531
}

// Cause 3: Array.find() returns undefined
const items = [1, 2, 3];
const found = items.find(x => x === 99);
console.log(found.toFixed()); // TS2531 (find returns T | undefined)

// Cause 4: Optional property access without null check
interface Config {
 host?: string;
}
function getHost(config: Config) {
 return config.host.toUpperCase(); // TS2531
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add a null check before accessing properties

// Wrong
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

// Correct
const el = document.getElementById("app");
if (el) {
 el.innerHTML = "Hello";
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

// Wrong
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

// Correct
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el?.innerHTML = "Hello";

Fix 3: Use non-null assertion when you’re sure

// Use ! only when you're certain the value is not null
const el = document.getElementById("app")!;
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

Fix 4: Use type narrowing in function parameters

function process(value: string | null) {
 if (value === null) {
 console.log("No value provided");
 return;
 }
 console.log(value.length); // OK — value is string
}

Examples

// Example 1: Document.querySelector returns Element | null
const btn = document.querySelector("button");
btn.addEventListener("click", handler); // TS2531

// Example 2: Object destructuring with optional values
interface Response {
 data?: { name: string };
}
function getName(res: Response) {
 return res.data.name; // TS2531: data is possibly undefined
}

Related Errors

  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — similar but for undefined instead of null.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignability issues.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — untyped values need narrowing.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2531 v2 — Object Is Possibly Null Fix
item.description:
TS2531 — Object Is Possibly Null (v2)

This variant covers TS2531 errors that arise from DOM queries, optional methods, and deeply nested null access patterns where strict null checks enforce safety.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2531: Object is possibly 'null'
  • TS2531: Cannot invoke an object which is possibly 'null'
  • TS2531: 'item' is possibly 'null'

Under strictNullChecks, TypeScript distinguishes between T, T | null, and T | undefined. Accessing a property or method on null will cause a runtime error, so TypeScript blocks it at compile time.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2532 — Object is possibly 'undefined'
item.description:
TS2532 — Object is possibly ‘undefined’

TS2532 occurs when you access a property or call a method on a value that TypeScript knows might be undefined. This is enforced when strictNullChecks is enabled.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Array.find can return undefined
const users = [{ name: "Alice" }, { name: "Bob" }];
const found = users.find(u => u.name === "Charlie");
console.log(found.name); // TS2532

// Cause 2: Object property access on possibly undefined
interface Config {
 db?: { host: string };
}
const config: Config = {};
console.log(config.db.host); // TS2532

// Cause 3: Map.get returns undefined
const map = new Map<string, number>();
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed(); // TS2532

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add undefined check

const found = users.find(u => u.name === "Charlie");
if (found) {
 console.log(found.name);
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

console.log(config.db?.host);

Fix 3: Use default value with nullish coalescing

const val = map.get("key") ?? 0;
val.toFixed();

Fix 4: Use non-null assertion

const found = users.find(u => u.name === "Alice")!;
console.log(found.name);

Related Errors

  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — null variant.
  • TS18048: X is possibly ‘undefined’ — variable-level check.
  • TS2571: Object is of type ‘unknown’ — unknown type variant.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2532 — Object Is Possibly 'undefined'
item.description:
TS2532 — Object is possibly ‘undefined’

TS2532 occurs when you access a property or call a method on a value that could be undefined. With strictNullChecks enabled, TypeScript requires you to verify that a value exists before using it.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Array element access without bounds checking
const arr: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
const value = arr[10];
value.toFixed(); // TS2532: Object is possibly 'undefined'

// Cause 2: Object property access on optional property
interface Config {
 database?: { host: string };
}
function getHost(config: Config) {
 return config.database.host; // TS2532: database is possibly undefined
}

// Cause 3: Map.get() returns V | undefined
const map = new Map<string, number>();
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed(); // TS2532

// Cause 4: Function return type includes undefined
function findUser(id: number): User | undefined {
 return users.find(u => u.id === id);
}
const user = findUser(1);
console.log(user.name); // TS2532

Solutions

Fix 1: Use optional chaining

// Wrong
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed();

// Correct
const val = map.get("key");
val?.toFixed();

Fix 2: Add a truthiness check

// Wrong
const user = findUser(1);
console.log(user.name);

// Correct
const user = findUser(1);
if (user) {
 console.log(user.name);
}

Fix 3: Use default values with nullish coalescing

const val = map.get("key") ?? 0;
val.toFixed(); // OK — val is number

Fix 4: Use non-null assertion when certain

// Use ! only when you're sure the value exists
const val = map.get("key")!;
val.toFixed();

Examples

// Example 1: Nested optional properties
interface Company {
 ceo?: {
 name?: string;
 };
}
function getCEOName(company: Company) {
 return company.ceo.name; // TS2532: ceo and name are possibly undefined
}
// Fix: company.ceo?.name

// Example 2: Promise.then with possible undefined
Promise.resolve(42).then(result => {
 result.toFixed(); // OK — result is number
});

Related Errors

  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — similar but for null instead of undefined.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignability.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — related to object shape mismatches.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2532 v2 — Object Is Possibly Undefined Fix
item.description:
TS2532 — Object Is Possibly Undefined (v2)

This variant of TS2532 covers scenarios involving async results, optional object properties, and Promise unwrapping where values may be undefined.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2532: Object is possibly 'undefined'
  • TS2532: 'name' is possibly 'undefined'
  • TS2532: Cannot access 'property' because value is being used before being assigned

TypeScript enforces that undefined is checked before property access. This prevents TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined at runtime.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2540 — Cannot Assign to Read-Only Property
item.description:
TS2540 — Cannot assign to ‘X’ because it is a read-only property

TS2540 occurs when you try to modify a value that’s marked as readonly, const, or comes from a Readonly<T> type. TypeScript prevents mutation of these values at compile time.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning to a const variable
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2540: Cannot assign to 'x' because it is a constant

// Cause 2: Modifying a readonly property
interface Config {
 readonly host: string;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost" };
config.host = "other.com"; // TS2540

// Cause 3: Modifying a readonly array
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // TS2540

// Cause 4: Modifying a property in Readonly<T>
interface User {
 name: string;
}
const user: Readonly<User> = { name: "Alice" };
user.name = "Bob"; // TS2540

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’let’ instead of ‘const’ for mutable variables

// Wrong
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2540

// Correct
let x = 10;
x = 20; // OK

Fix 2: Remove ‘readonly’ from the property

// Wrong
interface Config {
 readonly host: string;
}

// Correct — if mutation is intended
interface Config {
 host: string;
}

Fix 3: Create a mutable copy

// Wrong
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // TS2540

// Correct
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
const mutableArr = [...arr]; // Spread into mutable array
mutableArr.push(4);

Fix 4: Use a type assertion to bypass readonly (use carefully)

// Use only when you're certain mutation is safe
const config: Readonly<Config> = { host: "localhost" };
(config as Config).host = "other.com"; // Bypasses readonly

Examples

// Example 1: Readonly class property
class ApiClient {
 readonly baseUrl = "https://api.example.com";
}
const client = new ApiClient();
client.baseUrl = "https://other.com"; // TS2540

// Example 2: Object.freeze returns Readonly
const frozen = Object.freeze({ x: 1, y: 2 });
frozen.x = 10; // TS2540

// Example 3: Tuple assignment
const tuple: readonly [string, number] = ["hello", 42];
tuple[0] = "world"; // TS2540

Related Errors

  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — readonly prevents reading in some contexts.
  • TS2588: Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable — specific to const variables.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2542 — Cannot Read Property of Type
item.description:
TS2542 — Cannot read property ‘X’ of type ‘Y’

TS2542 occurs when you try to read a property from a type that doesn’t allow property access, typically with Readonly<T> types or when accessing properties on types that lack index signatures. This is less common than TS2540 and usually involves specific readonly constraints.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing property on wrong type
interface ReadonlyUser {
 readonly name: string;
 readonly age: number;
}
const user: ReadonlyUser = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
const data: ReadonlyUser = user;
// TS2542 if you try to access data on a type that doesn't expose it

// Cause 2: Indexed access on a readonly tuple
const tuple: readonly [string, number] = ["hello", 42];
const val: string = tuple[0]; // OK
tuple[0] = "world"; // TS2540 (write), not TS2542

// Cause 3: Using 'in' operator on type without property
type Empty = {};
const x: Empty = {};
"prop" in x; // No error, but...

// Cause 4: Readonly mapped type access
type ReadonlyObj = Readonly<{ x: number; y: number }>;
const obj: ReadonlyObj = { x: 1, y: 2 };

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct type that allows property access

// If you need to read properties, ensure the type exposes them
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.name); // OK

Fix 2: Use a mutable type when mutation is needed

// Wrong — readonly prevents writes
type ReadonlyUser = Readonly<{ name: string }>;

// Correct — use mutable type for mutable operations
type User = { name: string };

Fix 3: Cast to a type with the needed property

interface Base {
 id: number;
}
interface Extended extends Base {
 name: string;
}
const obj: Base = { id: 1 };
(obj as Extended).name; // Access via assertion

Fix 4: Use type narrowing for conditional access

function getValue(obj: unknown) {
 if (typeof obj === "object" && obj !== null && "name" in obj) {
 return (obj as { name: string }).name; // Safe access
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Readonly array methods
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.filter(x => x > 1); // OK — read-only methods allowed
arr.sort(); // TS2542 — mutating method not allowed on readonly array

// Example 2: Frozen object
const frozen = Object.freeze({ config: { debug: true } });
frozen.config = { debug: false }; // TS2542 / TS2540

Related Errors

  • TS2540: Cannot assign to read-only property — write access on readonly.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — property not declared.
  • TS2588: Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable — const assignment.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2551 — Property 'X' does not exist - did you mean 'Y'?
item.description:
TS2551 — Property ‘X’ does not exist - did you mean ‘Y’?

TS2551 is an enhanced version of TS2339 where TypeScript detects a property access that looks like a typo and suggests a similar property name that does exist on the type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in property name
interface User {
 firstName: string;
 lastName: string;
}
const user: User = { firstName: "Alice", lastName: "Smith" };
console.log(user.firsName); // TS2551: Did you mean 'firstName'?

// Cause 2: Wrong casing
const el = document.getElementById("myId");
el.innerHtml = "hello"; // TS2551: Did you mean 'innerHTML'?

// Cause 3: Missing import from destructured object
const { namee } = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2551: Did you mean 'name'?

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use the suggested property name

console.log(user.firstName); // correct spelling

Fix 2: Use the IDE suggestion

TypeScript language servers show quick fixes — accept the suggestion to rename automatically.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2551 — Property Does Not Exist (With Suggestions)
item.description:
TS2551 — Property ‘X’ does not exist (did you mean…)

TS2551 is a variant of TS2339 where TypeScript detects that the property doesn’t exist but finds a similarly-named property on the type. It usually includes a “did you mean…” suggestion, making it easier to fix typos.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in property name
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.nme); // TS2551: Property 'nme' does not exist. Did you mean 'name'?

// Cause 2: Typo in method name
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.filer(x => x > 1); // TS2551: Did you mean 'filter'?

// Cause 3: Wrong casing (camelCase vs PascalCase)
interface HttpResponse {
 statusCode: number;
}
const res: HttpResponse = { statusCode: 200 };
console.log(res.StatusCode); // TS2551: Did you mean 'statusCode'?

// Cause 4: Accessing property on wrong object
interface Car { make: string; }
interface Truck { bed: boolean; }
function processVehicle(v: Car) {
 console.log(v.bed); // TS2551: Property 'bed' does not exist on type 'Car'
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the suggested property name

// Wrong
console.log(user.nme);

// Correct — follow the "did you mean" suggestion
console.log(user.name);

Fix 2: Check casing conventions

// Wrong — PascalCase
console.log(res.StatusCode);

// Correct — camelCase
console.log(res.statusCode);

Fix 3: Verify you’re using the right object type

interface Car { make: string; }
interface Truck { bed: boolean; }

// If you need truck properties, accept Truck type
function processVehicle(v: Car | Truck) {
 if ("bed" in v) {
 console.log(v.bed); // OK — v is Truck
 }
}

Fix 4: Use IDE autocompletion to discover correct names

Most modern IDEs show available properties as you type, preventing these typos before compilation.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2552 — Cannot find name 'X'. Did you mean 'Y'?
item.description:
TS2552 — Cannot find name ‘X’. Did you mean ‘Y’?

TS2552 is an enhanced version of TS2304 where TypeScript detects an undefined identifier and suggests a similar name that does exist in the current scope.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in variable name
const userName = "Alice";
console.log(userNme); // TS2552: Did you mean 'userName'?

// Cause 2: Wrong import name
import { procesor } from "./cpu"; // TS2552: Did you mean 'processor'?

// Cause 3: Similar name exists in scope
const myFunction = () => {};
myFuntion(); // TS2552: Did you mean 'myFunction'?

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use the suggested name

console.log(userName); // correct spelling

Fix 2: Accept IDE suggestions

TypeScript language servers provide quick fixes for typos — accept the suggestion to auto-correct.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2552 — Cannot find name 'X'. Did you mean 'Y'?
item.description:
TS2552 — Cannot find name ‘X’. Did you mean ‘Y’?

TS2552 is an enhanced version of TS2304 that provides suggestions when an undefined identifier resembles an existing name.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in function name
function myFunction() {}
myFuntion(); // TS2552: Did you mean 'myFunction'?

// Cause 2: Wrong import name
import { procesor } from "./cpu"; // TS2552: Did you mean 'processor'?

// Cause 3: Similar variable exists
const userNme = "Alice"; // typo in declaration

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use the suggested name

myFunction(); // correct spelling

Fix 2: Fix the import

import { processor } from "./cpu";

Fix 3: Rename the declaration

const userName = "Alice";

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — no suggestions available.
  • TS2551: Property does not exist - did you mean — property variant.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2552 — Cannot Find Name, Did You Mean
item.description:
TS2552 — Cannot find name ‘X’, did you mean ‘Y’

TS2552 is an enhanced version of TS2304 where TypeScript not only reports that a name isn’t found, but also suggests a similar name that might be what you intended. This makes fixing typos much easier.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in variable name
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2552: Cannot find name 'useName'. Did you mean 'userName'?

// Cause 2: Typo in function name
function calculateTotal(items: number[]) {}
calculatTotal([1, 2, 3]); // TS2552: Did you mean 'calculateTotal'?

// Cause 3: Missing import with close alternative
import { UserService } from "./services";
const svc = new UsersService(); // TS2552: Did you mean 'UserService'?

// Cause 4: Wrong casing
const myArray = [1, 2, 3];
myarray.length; // TS2552: Did you mean 'myArray'?

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the suggested name

// Wrong
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2552

// Correct — follow the suggestion
console.log(userName);

Fix 2: Import the correct name

// Wrong
import { UserService } from "./services";
const svc = new UsersService(); // TS2552

// Correct
const svc = new UserService();

Fix 3: Check for case sensitivity

// Wrong — lowercase 'r'
const result = myarray.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);

// Correct — PascalCase or camelCase as defined
const result = myArray.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);

Fix 4: Ensure the identifier is in scope

// Wrong — using variable before it's defined
console.log(myVar);
const myVar = 10; // TS2552

// Correct
const myVar = 10;
console.log(myVar);

Examples

// Example 1: DOM API typo
const canvas = document.getElementById("myCanvas");
const ctx = canvas.getContex("2d"); // TS2552: Did you mean 'getContext'?

// Example 2: Array method typo
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
const evens = nums.fliter(x => x % 2 === 0); // TS2552: Did you mean 'filter'?

// Example 3: Class method typo
class Calculator {
 add(a: number, b: number) { return a + b; }
}
const calc = new Calculator();
calc.addd(1, 2); // TS2552: Did you mean 'add'?

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — no suggestion provided.
  • TS2551: Property does not exist — property-level suggestion.
  • TS1435: Unknown keyword or identifier — unrecognized keyword.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2554 — Expected X arguments but got Y
item.description:
TS2554 — Expected X arguments but got Y

TS2554 occurs when you call a function with a different number of arguments than its signature expects. This can mean too many or too few arguments.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing required arguments
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {
 return `${greeting}, ${name}`;
}
greet("Alice"); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 1

// Cause 2: Too many arguments
function add(a: number, b: number) {
 return a + b;
}
add(1, 2, 3); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 3

// Cause 3: Calling constructor with wrong count
class Point {
 constructor(x: number, y: number) {}
}
new Point(1); // TS2554

How to Fix

Fix 1: Provide the correct number of arguments

greet("Alice", "Hello");

Fix 2: Make parameters optional

function greet(name: string, greeting: string = "Hello") {
 return `${greeting}, ${name}`;
}
greet("Alice"); // OK

Fix 3: Use rest parameters for variadic functions

function log(...args: string[]) {
 args.forEach(a => console.log(a));
}
log("a", "b", "c"); // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type mismatch.
  • TS2794: Expected 1 arguments but got 0 — zero-argument variant.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload resolution failure.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2554 — Expected X Arguments But Got Y
item.description:
TS2554 — Expected X arguments, but got Y

TS2554 occurs when you call a function with a different number of arguments than its signature expects. Unlike JavaScript, TypeScript enforces exact argument counts based on the function’s type declaration.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Too few arguments
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {}
greet("Alice"); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 1

// Cause 2: Too many arguments
function add(a: number, b: number) {}
add(1, 2, 3); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 3

// Cause 3: Calling without required arguments
function fetchData(url: string, callback: Function) {}
fetchData(); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 0

// Cause 4: Spread operator passing wrong count
function log(a: string, b: string) {}
const args = ["hello"];
log(...args); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 1

Solutions

Fix 1: Provide the correct number of arguments

// Wrong
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {}
greet("Alice");

// Correct
greet("Alice", "Hello");

Fix 2: Make parameters optional if they aren’t always needed

// Wrong — greeting should be optional
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {}

// Correct
function greet(name: string, greeting?: string) {
 const g = greeting ?? "Hello";
 return `${g}, ${name}!`;
}
greet("Alice"); // OK
greet("Alice", "Hi"); // OK

Fix 3: Use rest parameters for variable argument count

// Wrong — fixed 2 args
function log(a: string, b: string) {}

// Correct — variable args
function log(...args: string[]) {}
log("hello"); // OK
log("hello", "world"); // OK

Fix 4: Remove extra arguments

// Wrong
add(1, 2, 3);

// Correct
add(1, 2);

Examples

// Example 1: Callback with wrong arity
function onComplete(success: boolean, message: string) {}
onComplete(true); // TS2554: Missing second argument

// Example 2: Spread with wrong count
const coords = [10, 20, 30] as const;
function point2D(x: number, y: number) {}
point2D(...coords); // TS2554: Expected 2, but got 3

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — right count, wrong types.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — no overload signature matches.
  • TS2551: Property does not exist — might mean you’re calling a non-existent method.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2571 — Object is of type 'unknown'
item.description:
TS2571 — Object is of type ‘unknown’

TS2571 occurs when you try to use a value of type unknown in a way that requires a more specific type. unknown requires explicit narrowing before use.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Unvalidated API response
async function fetchUser() {
 const res = await fetch("/api/user");
 const data = await res.json(); // data is 'unknown'
 console.log(data.name); // TS2571
}

// Cause 2: Unknown from catch
try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 console.log(e.message); // TS2571: e is 'unknown'
}

// Cause 3: Generic without constraint
function process<T>(value: T) {
 value.name; // TS2571 if T has no 'name'
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Type guard

if (typeof data === "object" && data !== null && "name" in data) {
 console.log((data as { name: string }).name);
}

Fix 2: Type assertion

const user = data as { name: string };
console.log(user.name);

Fix 3: Add generic constraint

function process<T extends { name: string }>(value: T) {
 console.log(value.name);
}

Fix 4: Use instanceof in catch

try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 if (e instanceof Error) {
 console.log(e.message);
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS18046: Variable is of type ‘unknown’ — variable unknown.
  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — null access.
  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — any type.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2578 — Type 'void' is not assignable
item.description:
TS2578 — Type ‘void’ is not assignable

TS2578 occurs when you try to use a void return value. void means the function does not return a meaningful value, and you cannot assign it to a variable expecting a real value.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning void to variable
function log(msg: string): void {
 console.log(msg);
}
const result = log("hello"); // TS2578: void is not assignable

// Cause 2: Using void in expression
function cleanup(): void {
 // ...
}
const val = cleanup() + 1; // TS2578

// Cause 3: Array of void
const tasks: void[] = [];
tasks.push(log("task")); // TS2578

How to Fix

Fix 1: Don’t use void return value

log("hello"); // just call it, don't capture result

Fix 2: Change return type

function log(msg: string): string {
 console.log(msg);
 return msg;
}
const result = log("hello"); // OK

Fix 3: Use void for callbacks only

const tasks: (() => void)[] = [];
tasks.push(() => log("task")); // OK — void callback

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type mismatch.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2580 — Cannot find name 'require'. Use import instead
item.description:
TS2580 — Cannot find name ‘require’. Use import instead

TS2580 occurs when you use require() in a TypeScript project configured for ES modules. TypeScript suggests using import statements instead.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using CommonJS require in ESM project
const express = require("express"); // TS2580

// Cause 2: require not typed
declare const require: any; // missing @types/node

// Cause 3: Wrong module system
// tsconfig.json has "module": "es2020" but code uses require

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use ES module import

import express from "express";

Fix 2: Install @types/node

npm install --save-dev @types/node

Fix 3: Use createRequire for dynamic imports

import { createRequire } from "module";
const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);
const pkg = require("package-name");

Fix 4: Switch module system

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "module": "commonjs"
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.
  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — type-only issues.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2588 — Cannot Assign to 'const' Variable
item.description:
TS2588 — Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable

TS2588 occurs when you try to reassign a variable declared with const. Unlike let, const variables cannot be reassigned after initialization. This is a fundamental JavaScript/TypeScript rule enforced at compile time.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Reassigning a const variable
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2588: Cannot assign to 'x' because it is a constant

// Cause 2: Trying to reassign in a loop
const counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++; // TS2588
}

// Cause 3: Reassigning a function parameter (const in destructuring)
function process({ id, name }: { id: number; name: string }) {
 id = 0; // TS2588: 'id' is a const
}

// Cause 4: Increment/decrement operator on const
const count = 0;
count++; // TS2588

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’let’ for variables that need reassignment

// Wrong
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2588

// Correct
let x = 10;
x = 20; // OK

Fix 2: Use ’let’ for counters

// Wrong
const counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++; // TS2588
}

// Correct
let counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++;
}

Fix 3: Use a new variable instead of reassignment

// Wrong
const count = 0;
count++;

// Correct — create a new value
const count = 0;
const newCount = count + 1;

Fix 4: Use mutable objects with const

// Const prevents reassignment, not mutation
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // OK — mutation is allowed
arr = [5, 6, 7]; // TS2588 — reassignment is not

// If you need to "replace" the array
const arr2 = [...arr, 4]; // New array, same reference

Examples

// Example 1: React state with const
function Counter() {
 const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
 // count++; // TS2588 — can't reassign const
 setCount(count + 1); // Correct — use setter
}

// Example 2: Trying to swap values
let a = 1;
let b = 2;
const temp = a;
a = b;
b = temp; // OK — temp is const but we're not reassigning it
// But: temp = 5; // TS2588

// Example 3: Destructured const
const [first, second] = [1, 2];
// first = 10; // TS2588

Related Errors

  • TS2540: Cannot assign to read-only property — readonly object properties.
  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — readonly access issues.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignment issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2614 — Module has no exported member
item.description:
TS2614 — Module has no exported member ‘X’

TS2614 occurs when you import a member from a module that does not export that name. The member may not exist, may not be exported, or may have been renamed.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Importing non-exported member
// file: utils.ts
function helper() {} // not exported

// file: app.ts
import { helper } from "./utils"; // TS2614

// Cause 2: Wrong export name
// file: utils.ts
export function process() {}

// file: app.ts
import { proces } from "./utils"; // TS2614: typo

// Cause 3: Named vs default export confusion
// file: utils.ts
export default function helper() {}

// file: app.ts
import { helper } from "./utils"; // TS2614: should use default

How to Fix

Fix 1: Export the member

// file: utils.ts
export function helper() {} // add export

Fix 2: Fix the import name

import { process } from "./utils"; // correct name

Fix 3: Use correct import style

// For default export
import helper from "./utils";

// For named export
import { helper } from "./utils";

Related Errors

  • TS2305: Module has no exported member — similar error.
  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — type-only usage.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2618 — A class may only extend another class or null
item.description:
TS2618 — A class may only extend another class or null

TS2618 occurs when a class tries to extend something that is not a class (e.g., an interface, type, or object).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extending an interface
interface Animal {
 name: string;
}
class Dog extends Animal {} // TS2618

// Cause 2: Extending a type alias
type Base = {
 value: number;
};
class Derived extends Base {} // TS2618

// Cause 3: Extending an expression
const base = { name: "base" };
class Derived extends base {} // TS2618

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use implements instead

interface Animal {
 name: string;
}
class Dog implements Animal {
 name = "Rex";
}

Fix 2: Use class instead of interface

class Animal {
 name: string = "";
}
class Dog extends Animal {
 name = "Rex";
}

Fix 3: Use mixin pattern

type Constructor<T = {}> = new (...args: any[]) => T;
function Timestamped<TBase extends Constructor>(Base: TBase) {
 return class extends Base {
 createdAt = new Date();
 };
}

Related Errors

  • TS2320: Interface cannot extend — interface extension.
  • TS2461: Interface incorrectly extends — interface type conflict.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — class implementation.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2673 — X cannot be instantiated
item.description:
TS2673 — Member ‘X’ cannot be instantiated with ’new’

TS2673 occurs when you try to use new on an abstract class or a type that does not have a construct signature.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Instantiating abstract class
abstract class Shape {
 abstract area(): number;
}
const s = new Shape(); // TS2673

// Cause 2: Interface without construct signature
interface Factory {
 create(): void;
}
const f = new Factory(); // TS2673

// Cause 3: Type alias not constructable
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
const p = new Point(); // TS2673

How to Fix

Fix 1: Instantiate concrete subclass

class Circle extends Shape {
 area() { return Math.PI * 4; }
}
const c = new Circle();

Fix 2: Add construct signature

interface Factory {
 new (): { create(): void };
}

Fix 3: Use object literal

const p: Point = { x: 1, y: 2 };

Related Errors

  • TS2511: Cannot create instance of abstract class — abstract instantiation.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist — property access errors.
  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — non-callable expression.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2683 — 'this' Implicitly Has Type 'any'
item.description:
TS2683 — ’this’ implicitly has type ‘any’

TS2683 occurs when noImplicitThis is enabled (part of strict mode) and TypeScript can’t determine the type of this in a function. This commonly happens with standalone functions, callbacks, and untyped method references.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Standalone function without 'this' parameter
function greet() {
 console.log(this.name); // TS2683: 'this' implicitly has type 'any'
}

// Cause 2: Callback losing 'this' context
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(function () {
 this.seconds++; // TS2683: 'this' is 'any' inside callback
 }, 1000);
 }
}

// Cause 3: Passing method as callback without binding
class Logger {
 log() {
 console.log("logged");
 }
}
const fn = new Logger().log;
fn(); // TS2683: 'this' is 'any'

// Cause 4: Object literal with untyped 'this'
const obj = {
 value: 10,
 getValue() {
 return this.value; // TS2683 if noImplicitThis is on
 }
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Add a ’this’ parameter to the function

// Wrong
function greet() {
 console.log(this.name);
}

// Correct
interface ThisContext {
 name: string;
}
function greet(this: ThisContext) {
 console.log(this.name);
}

Fix 2: Use arrow functions to preserve ’this'

// Wrong
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(function () {
 this.seconds++;
 }, 1000);
 }
}

// Correct — arrow function captures 'this' lexically
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(() => {
 this.seconds++;
 }, 1000);
 }
}

Fix 3: Use .bind() when passing methods as callbacks

class Logger {
 log() {
 console.log("logged");
 }
}
const logger = new Logger();
const fn = logger.log.bind(logger);
fn();

Fix 4: Type ’this’ in arrow function properties

const obj = {
 value: 10,
 getValue: function (this: { value: number }) {
 return this.value;
 }
};

Examples

// Example 1: jQuery-style callback
$(".button").on("click", function () {
 $(this).addClass("active"); // TS2683: 'this' is HTMLElement, not any
 // Fix: add 'this: HTMLElement' parameter
});

// Example 2: Event handler
document.addEventListener("click", function (e) {
 console.log(this.id); // TS2683: 'this' is Document, not HTMLElement
});

Related Errors

  • TS2684: The ’this’ context of type ‘X’ is not assignable — ’this’ type mismatch.
  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — other implicit any issues.
  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructured implicit any.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2683 — Implicitly has type 'any'
item.description:
TS2683 — Implicitly has type ‘any’

TS2683 occurs when noImplicitThis is enabled and TypeScript cannot determine the type of this in a function. Without explicit typing, this defaults to any.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Regular function without this type
function logName() {
 console.log(this.name); // TS2683: 'this' implicitly has type 'any'
}

// Cause 2: Callback losing context
class Person {
 name = "Alice";
 greet() {
 setTimeout(function() {
 console.log(this.name); // TS2683
 }, 100);
 }
}

// Cause 3: Object method without explicit this
const obj = {
 value: 42,
 getValue() {
 return this.value; // TS2683
 }
};

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use arrow functions

class Person {
 name = "Alice";
 greet() {
 setTimeout(() => {
 console.log(this.name); // OK — arrow function inherits this
 }, 100);
 }
}

Fix 2: Annotate ’this’ parameter

function logName(this: { name: string }) {
 console.log(this.name);
}

Fix 3: Use explicitly typed this

interface Loggable {
 name: string;
}
function logName(this: Loggable) {
 console.log(this.name);
}

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — parameter implicit any.
  • TS2684: The ’this’ context of type is not assignable — this type mismatch.
  • TS18046: Variable is of type ‘unknown’ — unknown type.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2684 — The 'this' Context Type Is Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2684 — The ’this’ context of type ‘X’ is not assignable

TS2684 occurs when the actual this context in a function doesn’t match the declared this parameter type. This is stricter than TS2683 — TypeScript knows what this is, but it doesn’t match what the function expects.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: 'this' parameter type doesn't match the object
interface Logger {
 log: (msg: string) => void;
 prefix: string;
}
function doLog(this: Logger, msg: string) {
 console.log(this.prefix + msg);
}
const obj = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };
doLog.call(obj, "hello"); // TS2684: 'this' context mismatch

// Cause 2: Method called on wrong object type
class Animal {
 speak() {}
}
class Robot {
 code() {}
}
const robot = new Robot();
const speak = Animal.prototype.speak;
speak.call(robot); // TS2684: Robot is not Animal

// Cause 3: Arrow function captures wrong 'this'
class Outer {
 value = 10;
 method() {
 const inner = {
 value: 20,
 getThis: () => this.value // Captures Outer's 'this', not inner's
 };
 }
}

// Cause 4: Calling method with incompatible receiver
interface HasId {
 id: number;
}
function getId(this: HasId) {
 return this.id;
}
getId.call({ name: "Alice" }); // TS2684: missing 'id'

Solutions

Fix 1: Ensure the object matches the ’this’ parameter type

interface Logger {
 log: (msg: string) => void;
 prefix: string;
}

// Wrong — obj doesn't fully match Logger
const obj = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };

// Correct — ensure full type match
const obj: Logger = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };
doLog.call(obj, "hello");

Fix 2: Use .bind() with the correct type

class Animal {
 speak() { return "speaking"; }
}
const animal = new Animal();
const speak = animal.speak.bind(animal);
speak(); // OK

Fix 3: Use arrow functions to capture the right ’this’

class Outer {
 value = 10;
 method() {
 // Arrow function captures Outer's this
 const getOuterValue = () => this.value;
 return getOuterValue();
 }
}

Fix 4: Match ’this’ parameter to actual context

interface HasId {
 id: number;
}

// Wrong — calling with object that doesn't have 'id'
getId.call({ name: "Alice" });

// Correct — pass object with required shape
getId.call({ id: 1, name: "Alice" });

Examples

// Example 1: React class component
class MyComponent {
 state = { count: 0 };
 handleClick() {
 this.setState({ count: this.state.count + 1 }); // 'this' must be MyComponent
 }
}

// Example 2: forEach callback with 'this' parameter
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.forEach(function (item) {
 console.log(this); // 'this' must match declared type
}, someObject);

Related Errors

  • TS2683: ’this’ implicitly has type ‘any’ — ’this’ has no type at all.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — function argument mismatches.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2688 — Type Has No Matching Call Signature
item.description:
TS2688 — Type ‘X’ has no matching call signature

TS2688 occurs when you try to call a value as a function, but its type doesn’t have a call signature that matches the arguments provided. This differs from TS2349 (not callable at all) — here the type might be callable, but not with your specific arguments.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling with wrong argument types
type Callback = (x: number) => void;
const fn: Callback = (x) => console.log(x);
fn("hello"); // TS2688: string is not assignable to number

// Cause 2: Calling union type that isn't callable in all cases
type MaybeFn = (() => void) | null;
const value: MaybeFn = null;
value(); // TS2688: 'null' has no call signature

// Cause 3: Calling a type that isn't a function
type Config = { host: string; port: number };
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000 };
config(); // TS2688: Config has no call signature

// Cause 4: Generic type without call signature
type Container<T> = { value: T };
const container: Container<number> = { value: 42 };
container(); // TS2688

Solutions

Fix 1: Provide arguments that match the call signature

// Wrong
type Callback = (x: number) => void;
const fn: Callback = (x) => console.log(x);
fn("hello"); // TS2688

// Correct
fn(42);

Fix 2: Narrow the type before calling

// Wrong
type MaybeFn = (() => void) | null;
const value: MaybeFn = null;
value(); // TS2688

// Correct
if (value) {
 value(); // OK — value is () => void
}

Fix 3: Use a type guard or typeof check

type ConfigOrFunction = Config | (() => void);
const value: ConfigOrFunction = getSomething();

if (typeof value === "function") {
 value(); // OK — TypeScript knows it's callable
}

Fix 4: Ensure the type has a call signature

// Wrong
type Config = { host: string };
const config: Config = { host: "localhost" };
config(); // TS2688

// Correct — define a callable interface
interface Factory {
 (): Config;
 create(): Config;
}

Examples

// Example 1: Array method type mismatch
const nums: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
nums.includes("1"); // TS2688: string not assignable to number

// Example 2: Promise.resolve type
const p: Promise<number> = Promise.resolve(42);
p.then("not a function"); // TS2688

Related Errors

  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — value has no call signature at all.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — argument type mismatch.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload resolution failure.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2693 — 'X' only refers to a type
item.description:
TS2693 — ‘X’ only refers to a type but is being used as a value here

TS2693 occurs when you try to use a type-only declaration as a runtime value. Types exist only at compile time and cannot be used at runtime.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using interface as value
interface User {
 name: string;
}
const u = new User(); // TS2693: 'User' only refers to a type

// Cause 2: Using type alias as value
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
const p = Point(); // TS2693

// Cause 3: Importing type as value
import type { Config } from "./config";
const c = new Config(); // TS2693: Config is type-only

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use class instead of interface

class User {
 name: string;
 constructor(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
 }
}
const u = new User("Alice");

Fix 2: Use typeof for type operations

const obj = { x: 1, y: 2 };
type Point = typeof obj; // type from value

Fix 3: Import as value

import { Config } from "./config"; // remove 'type'
const c = new Config();

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property is type-only import — type-only import access.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — non-callable expression.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2694 — Namespace 'X' has no exported member 'Y'
item.description:
TS2694 — Namespace ‘X’ has no exported member ‘Y’

TS2694 occurs when you access a member of a namespace that does not export that name. Only exported members are accessible from outside the namespace.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing non-exported member
namespace MathUtils {
 const PI = 3.14; // not exported
 export function square(x: number) { return x * x; }
}
console.log(MathUtils.PI); // TS2694

// Cause 2: Typo in member name
namespace Colors {
 export const Red = "red";
}
console.log(Colors.Read); // TS2694: typo

// Cause 3: Wrong namespace
namespace A {
 export const value = 1;
}
namespace B {
