 export const value = 2;
}
console.log(A.value); // OK
console.log(B.value); // OK

How to Fix

Fix 1: Export the member

namespace MathUtils {
 export const PI = 3.14; // add export
 export function square(x: number) { return x * x; }
}

Fix 2: Fix the member name

console.log(Colors.Red);

Fix 3: Use import in namespace

namespace MyNamespace {
 import Utils = OtherNamespace.Utils;
 export function use() { Utils.process(); }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2305: Module has no exported member — module variant.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2503: Cannot find namespace — namespace not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2707 — X is a type but not a module
item.description:
TS2707 — Generic type ‘X’ from module ‘“Y”’ cannot be used without an import

TS2707 occurs when you try to import a type from a file that is not a module (does not have any top-level import/export statements).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: File is not a module
// file: types.ts (no export statements)
interface User {
 name: string;
}

// file: app.ts
import { User } from "./types"; // TS2707

// Cause 2: Wrong file extension
import { helper } from "./utils.js"; // TS2707 if utils.ts has no exports

// Cause 3: Missing exports
// file: helpers.ts
function helper() {} // not exported
import { helper } from "./helpers"; // TS2707

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add export to the file

// file: types.ts
export interface User {
 name: string;
}

Fix 2: Use triple-slash reference

/// <reference path="./types.d.ts" />

Fix 3: Use ambient module declaration

// In a .d.ts file
declare module "./types" {
 export interface User {
 name: string;
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2306: File is not a module — similar error.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.
  • TS2503: Cannot find namespace — namespace not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2717 — Non-abstract class does not implement
item.description:
TS2717 — Non-abstract class ‘X’ does not implement inherited abstract member

TS2717 occurs when a concrete (non-abstract) class extends an abstract class or implements an interface but fails to provide implementations for all abstract members.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing abstract method implementation
abstract class Shape {
 abstract area(): number;
}

class Circle extends Shape {
 radius: number;
 constructor(r: number) {
 super();
 this.radius = r;
 }
 // missing area() — TS2717
}

// Cause 2: Incomplete interface implementation
interface Repository {
 find(id: number): any;
 save(entity: any): void;
}

class UserRepository implements Repository {
 find(id: number) {
 return null;
 }
 // missing save() — TS2717
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Implement all abstract members

class Circle extends Shape {
 radius: number;
 constructor(r: number) {
 super();
 this.radius = r;
 }
 area(): number {
 return Math.PI * this.radius ** 2;
 }
}

Fix 2: Use abstract in concrete class

abstract class Circle extends Shape {
 radius: number;
 constructor(r: number) {
 super();
 this.radius = r;
 }
 // area() left abstract — subclass must implement
}

Related Errors

  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — interface implementation.
  • TS2720: Class extends abstract — abstract member conflict.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined member.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2720 — Class 'X' defines instance member 'Y'
item.description:
TS2720 — Class ‘X’ defines instance member ‘Y’ but previously required static member

TS2720 occurs when a class extends an abstract class but implements a member with the wrong static/instance modifier.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Instance vs static mismatch
abstract class Base {
 abstract static create(): Base;
}

class Derived extends Base {
 create(): Base { // TS2720: instance member, expected static
 return new Derived();
 }
}

// Cause 2: Wrong modifier
abstract class Logger {
 abstract log(msg: string): void;
}

class ConsoleLogger extends Logger {
 static log(msg: string) {} // TS2720: static, expected instance
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Match the static/instance modifier

abstract class Base {
 static create(): Base;
}

class Derived extends Base {
 static create(): Base {
 return new Derived();
 }
}

Fix 2: Use correct instance method

class ConsoleLogger extends Logger {
 log(msg: string) {
 console.log(msg);
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2717: Non-abstract class does not implement — missing implementation.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — interface mismatch.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined member.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2722 — Cannot invoke object which is possibly 'callable'
item.description:
TS2722 — Cannot invoke an object which is possibly ‘callable’

TS2722 occurs when you try to call a value that might be a function but could also be null or undefined.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Nullable callback
type Callback = (() => void) | null;
let cb: Callback = getCallback();
cb(); // TS2722: cb is possibly null

// Cause 2: Optional function property
interface Config {
 onError?: (err: Error) => void;
}
const config: Config = {};
config.onError(new Error("oops")); // TS2722

// Cause 3: Function from nullable source
const handler = document.getElementById("btn")?.onclick;
handler(); // TS2722

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add null check

if (cb) {
 cb();
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

cb?.();

Fix 3: Use non-null assertion

cb!();

Fix 4: Provide default function

const handler = config.onError ?? (() => {});
handler(new Error("oops"));

Related Errors

  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — null property access.
  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — undefined property access.
  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — non-callable expression.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2724 — X does not exist in the scope
item.description:
TS2724 — Module has no exported member ‘X’ or it was removed

TS2724 occurs when you import a member that was removed from a module or never existed. This often happens after package updates.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Member removed in package update
import { oldFunction } from "some-package"; // TS2724 if removed

// Cause 2: Wrong export name
import { procesor } from "./cpu"; // TS2724

// Cause 3: Breaking change in major version
import { Component } from "old-framework"; // TS2724 after migration

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check package changelog

npm info some-package --json
# Check what was renamed or removed

Fix 2: Fix the import name

import { processor } from "./cpu";

Fix 3: Use replacement API

import { newFunction } from "some-package"; // use the replacement

Related Errors

  • TS2305: Module has no exported member — similar error.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.
  • TS2694: Namespace has no exported member — namespace variant.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2740 — Type Is Missing Properties
item.description:
TS2740 — Type ‘X’ is missing the following properties

TS2740 occurs when an object literal or value doesn’t have all the required properties that the target type demands. This is closely related to TS2741 but typically involves more complex type structures or when assigning to a variable rather than a function parameter.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing properties in object literal assignment
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2740: missing 'age' and 'email'

// Cause 2: Missing properties from intersection type
interface A { a: string; }
interface B { b: number; }
type AB = A & B;
const ab: AB = { a: "hello" }; // TS2740: missing 'b'

// Cause 3: Class not implementing all interface members
interface Repository {
 find(id: number): any;
 save(entity: any): void;
 delete(id: number): void;
}
class SimpleRepo implements Repository {
 find(id: number) { return null; } // TS2740: missing 'save' and 'delete'
}

// Cause 4: Missing properties from generic constraint
interface Form<T> {
 values: T;
 errors: Record<keyof T, string>;
}
const form: Form<{ name: string }> = {
 values: { name: "" }
 // TS2740: missing 'errors'
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Add all missing properties

// Wrong
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

// Correct
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30, email: "alice@example.com" };

Fix 2: Use Partial if properties are optional

// Wrong
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2740

// Correct — use Partial for optional
const user: Partial<User> = { name: "Alice" };

Fix 3: Implement all interface members in classes

// Wrong
class SimpleRepo implements Repository {
 find(id: number) { return null; }
}

// Correct
class SimpleRepo implements Repository {
 find(id: number) { return null; }
 save(entity: any) { /* ... */ }
 delete(id: number) { /* ... */ }
}

Fix 4: Provide defaults or spread missing properties

const defaults = { age: 0, email: "" };
const user: User = { ...defaults, name: "Alice" };

Examples

// Example 1: React state type
interface State {
 count: number;
 loading: boolean;
 error: string | null;
}
this.setState({ count: 0 }); // TS2740: missing 'loading' and 'error'

// Fix: use Partial
this.setState({ count: 0 } as Partial<State>);

// Example 2: API response type
interface ApiResponse {
 status: number;
 data: unknown;
 headers: Record<string, string>;
}
const res: ApiResponse = { status: 200, data: {} }; // TS2740

Related Errors

  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — similar but for single property.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2741 — Property 'X' is missing in type 'Y'
item.description:
TS2741 — Property ‘X’ is missing in type ‘Y’

TS2741 occurs when you assign an object to a type that expects properties not present in the source object. This is a specific variant of type assignability focused on missing properties.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing required property
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}

const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 }; // TS2741: 'email' is missing

// Cause 2: Partial return value
function getUser(): User {
 return { name: "Alice", age: 30 }; // TS2741: 'email' is missing
}

// Cause 3: Object literal missing properties
const config: { host: string; port: number } = { host: "localhost" }; // TS2741

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add the missing property

const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30, email: "alice@example.com" };

Fix 2: Make the property optional in the interface

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email?: string; // optional
}

Fix 3: Use Partial for flexible types

const user: Partial<User> = { name: "Alice" }; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general assignability.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — class interface mismatch.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist — extra properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2741 — Property Is Missing in Type
item.description:
TS2741 — Property ‘X’ is missing in type ‘Y’

TS2741 occurs when an object literal or value is missing required properties that the target type demands. This is a specific variant of TS2322 focused on missing properties in object shapes.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing required property in object literal
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 }; // TS2741: Property 'email' is missing

// Cause 2: Missing property in function return
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
 debug: boolean;
}
function getConfig(): Config {
 return { host: "localhost", port: 3000 }; // TS2741: 'debug' is missing
}

// Cause 3: Missing property when implementing an interface
interface Serializable {
 serialize(): string;
 validate(): boolean;
}
class MyClass implements Serializable {
 serialize() { return ""; } // TS2741: 'validate' is missing
}

// Cause 4: Missing property in array of objects
const users: User[] = [
 { name: "Alice", age: 30 }, // TS2741: 'email' is missing
];

Solutions

Fix 1: Add all required properties

// Wrong
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

// Correct
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30, email: "alice@example.com" };

Fix 2: Make optional properties with ?

// If email isn't always needed, mark it optional
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email?: string; // Optional
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 }; // OK

Fix 3: Use default values or spread

function getConfig(): Config {
 const defaults = { debug: false };
 return { ...defaults, host: "localhost", port: 3000 };
}

Fix 4: Implement all interface members

class MyClass implements Serializable {
 serialize() { return ""; }
 validate() { return true; } // Implement missing method
}

Examples

// Example 1: React component props
interface ButtonProps {
 onClick: () => void;
 label: string;
 variant: "primary" | "secondary";
}
// Missing 'variant' prop
<Button onClick={handler} label="Click" />; // TS2741

// Example 2: API response type
interface ApiResponse {
 status: number;
 data: unknown;
 timestamp: string;
}
const res: ApiResponse = { status: 200, data: {} }; // TS2741

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability including extra properties.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.
  • TS2749: Refers to a value, used as a type — value vs type confusion.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2749 — Refers to a Value But Used as a Type
item.description:
TS2749 — ‘X’ refers to a value, but is being used as a type here

TS2749 occurs when you use a value (like a class instance or variable) in a type position where TypeScript expects a type. This typically happens when importing a value instead of a type, or confusing class instances with class types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a class instance as a type annotation
class User {
 name: string = "";
}
const user = new User();
type MyType = typeof user; // This is the instance type

let x: user; // TS2749: 'user' refers to a value, but is being used as a type

// Cause 2: Importing a value instead of a type
import MyComponent from "./MyComponent";
type Props = MyComponent; // TS2749 if MyComponent is a value

// Cause 3: Using a variable name as a type
const config = { host: "localhost" };
type T = config; // TS2749

// Cause 4: Forgetting to use 'typeof' for value-to-type conversion
function process(input: string) {}
const fn = process;
type FnType = fn; // TS2749

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’typeof’ to convert a value to its type

class User {
 name: string = "";
}
const user = new User();

// Wrong
let x: user; // TS2749

// Correct
let x: typeof user; // OK — x has the same shape as user

// Better — use the class name directly
let x: User; // OK — this is the instance type

Fix 2: Import types using ‘import type’

// Wrong — imports the value
import MyComponent from "./MyComponent";

// Correct — imports only the type
import type MyComponent from "./MyComponent";
type Props = MyComponent; // OK

Fix 3: Use the class name as the type

class User {
 name: string = "";
}

// Wrong — use the class name, not an instance
const user = new User();
function process(u: user) {} // TS2749

// Correct
function process(u: User) {} // OK

Fix 4: Extract the type from a variable using typeof

const config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000 };

// Wrong
type T = config;

// Correct
type T = typeof config; // { host: string; port: number }

Examples

// Example 1: Common mistake with module exports
// utils.ts
export const helper = () => {};
export type HelperType = { name: string };

// app.ts
import { helper, HelperType } from "./utils";
type T = helper; // TS2749 — helper is a value
type T2 = HelperType; // OK — HelperType is a type

// Example 2: Using enum as a type
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
type T = Color; // TS2749 — Color is a value (enum)
type T2 = typeof Color; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type issues.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module import issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2761 — X can only be declared as optional property
item.description:
TS2761 — ‘X’ can only be declared as optional property

TS2761 occurs when a property in an intersection type is required in one type but optional in another, creating a conflict.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Conflicting optional/required in intersection
interface A {
 name: string;
}
interface B {
 name?: string;
}
type C = A & B; // TS2761: 'name' can only be optional

// Cause 2: Partial & required conflict
type Strict = { id: number };
type Loose = { id?: string };
type Both = Strict & Loose; // TS2761

// Cause 3: Merged interfaces with different optionality
interface Base {
 value: string;
}
interface Extended {
 value?: number;
}
type Merged = Base & Extended; // TS2761

How to Fix

Fix 1: Make properties consistent

interface A {
 name?: string;
}
interface B {
 name?: string;
}
type C = A & B; // OK — both optional

Fix 2: Use union type instead

type Result = A | B; // union, not intersection

Fix 3: Separate conflicting properties

interface Combined {
 name: string;
 altName?: string;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing property.
  • TS2356: Type has no properties in common — incompatible types.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2762 — JSX Element Missing Closing Tag
item.description:
TS2762 — JSX element ‘X’ has no corresponding closing tag

TS2762 occurs when a JSX element is opened but never closed. JSX requires all elements to either be self-closing (<Component />) or have a matching closing tag (</Component>).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing closing tag
function App() {
 return <div>Hello; // TS2762: no closing </div>
}

// Cause 2: Missing self-closing slash
function App() {
 return <img src="photo.jpg">; // TS2762: should be <img ... />
}

// Cause 3: Mismatched tags
function App() {
 return (
 <div>
 <span>Hello</div> // TS2762: </span> missing
 </div>
 );
}

// Cause 4: Unclosed component
function App() {
 return <MyComponent props={value}; // TS2762: missing /> or </MyComponent>
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add the closing tag

// Wrong
return <div>Hello;

// Correct
return <div>Hello</div>;

Fix 2: Use self-closing syntax for void elements

// Wrong
return <img src="photo.jpg">;

// Correct
return <img src="photo.jpg" />;

Fix 3: Ensure all nested tags are properly closed

// Wrong
return (
 <div>
 <span>Hello</div>
 </div>
);

// Correct
return (
 <div>
 <span>Hello</span>
 </div>
);

Fix 4: Close custom components properly

// Wrong
return <MyComponent props={value}>;

// Correct — self-closing
return <MyComponent props={value} />;

// Or with children
return (
 <MyComponent props={value}>
 <p>Child content</p>
 </MyComponent>
);

Examples

// Example 1: Conditional rendering without closing
function App({ show }: { show: boolean }) {
 return (
 <div>
 {show && <Alert message="Warning" // TS2762
 }
 </div>
 );
}

// Fix:
return (
 <div>
 {show && <Alert message="Warning" />}
 </div>
);

// Example 2: Fragment without closing
function App() {
 return (
 <>
 <p>First</p>
 <p>Second</p>
 // TS2762: missing </>
 );
}

Related Errors

  • TS2786: ‘X’ cannot be used as a JSX component — JSX component type issues.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader syntax error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2769 — No overload matches this call
item.description:
TS2769 — No overload matches this call

TS2769 occurs when you call a function with overloaded signatures and none of the overloads match the provided arguments. TypeScript cannot find a single overload that accepts your argument types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Arguments don't match any overload
function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: number): number;
function process(input: string | number): string | number {
 return input;
}
process(true); // TS2769: no overload matches

// Cause 2: Spread arguments that don't fit
function log(msg: string): void;
function log(msg: string, level: number): void;
function log(...args: any[]): void {}
log("hello", "world"); // TS2769: 'string' not assignable to 'number'

// Cause 3: Generic constraint failure
function wrap<T extends HTMLElement>(el: T): T { return el; }
wrap(document.getElementById("myId")); // TS2769 if null

How to Fix

Fix 1: Match an existing overload

process("hello"); // matches first overload
process(42); // matches second overload

Fix 2: Add a new overload for your use case

function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: number): number;
function process(input: boolean): string; // add boolean overload
function process(input: string | number | boolean): string | number {
 return input;
}

Fix 3: Fix the argument type

const el = document.getElementById("myId");
if (el) {
 wrap(el); // el is now HTMLElement, not null
}

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — single-signature argument mismatch.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — wrong argument count.
  • TS2769: No overload matches — complex overload failure.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2769 — No Overload Matches This Call
item.description:
TS2769 — No overload matches this call

TS2769 occurs when a function has multiple overload signatures but none of them match the arguments you provided. TypeScript checks each overload and if none are compatible, it reports this error.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Arguments don't match any overload
function process(value: string): void;
function process(value: number): void;
function process(value: boolean): void;
function process(value: any) {}

process({ key: "value" }); // TS2769: No overload matches this call

// Cause 2: Wrong argument types in overloaded function
function createElement(tag: "div"): HTMLDivElement;
function createElement(tag: "span"): HTMLSpanElement;
function createElement(tag: string): HTMLElement;
function createElement(tag: string) {}

createElement("canvas"); // TS2769: 'canvas' doesn't match specific overloads

// Cause 3: Missing arguments in some overloads
function fetch(url: string): Promise<Response>;
function fetch(url: string, init: RequestInit): Promise<Response>;
function fetch(url: string, init?: RequestInit) {}

fetch(); // TS2769: No overload matches

// Cause 4: Type mismatch with generic constraints
function merge<T extends object>(a: T, b: T): T & T {
 return { ...a, ...b };
}
merge("a", "b"); // TS2769: string is not an object

Solutions

Fix 1: Match one of the defined overload signatures

// Wrong
function process(value: string): void;
function process(value: number): void;
function process(value: any) {}
process({ key: "value" });

// Correct — use a valid type
process("hello"); // matches string overload
process(42); // matches number overload

Fix 2: Add a catch-all overload at the end

// Add an overload that accepts any type
function createElement(tag: "div"): HTMLDivElement;
function createElement(tag: "span"): HTMLSpanElement;
function createElement(tag: string): HTMLElement;
function createElement(tag: string) {} // catch-all

Fix 3: Use type assertions to satisfy an overload

const input = document.getElementById("canvas") as HTMLCanvasElement;

Fix 4: Restructure overloads to cover more cases

// Instead of specific overloads, use union types
function process(value: string | number | boolean) {}

Examples

// Example 1: React setState overload mismatch
interface State {
 count: number;
}
class Component {
 setState(state: Partial<State>) {}
}
this.setState({ count: "5" }); // TS2769

// Example 2: Array.reduce with wrong initial value
const nums = [1, 2, 3];
nums.reduce((acc, val) => acc + val); // TS2769 in some TS versions

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — no overloads involved, just a single signature.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments, but got Y — wrong argument count.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — assignment rather than function call.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2769 v2 — No Overload Matches This Call Fix
item.description:
TS2769 — No Overload Matches This Call (v2)

This variant of TS2769 covers complex overload failures involving overloaded functions from libraries like React, Express, or DOM APIs where the combination of arguments doesn’t match any available signature.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2769: No overload matches this call. Overload 1 of 12 ...
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call. The last overload ...
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call. Expected 2 arguments, but got 3

A function has multiple call signatures (overloads). TypeScript checks each one and finds that none match the arguments you provided. The error lists all available overloads.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2786 — Cannot Be Used as a JSX Component
item.description:
TS2786 — ‘X’ cannot be used as a JSX component

TS2786 occurs when you use a value as a JSX component, but its type doesn’t match the expected JSX component interface. This commonly happens with React components that have incorrect prop types, missing type definitions, or when using third-party component libraries.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Function component with wrong return type
function MyComponent() {
 return null; // Returns null, which is valid JSX
}
// But if the function signature is wrong:
const MyComponent = () => 42; // TS2786: number is not a valid JSX element

// Cause 2: Using a non-component as a JSX element
const notAComponent = "hello";
return <notAComponent />; // TS2786

// Cause 3: Missing React types
// If @types/react is not installed
const App = () => <div>Hello</div>; // TS2786 if React types missing

// Cause 4: Class component not extending React.Component
class MyComponent {
 render() {
 return <div>Hello</div>; // TS2786: not a valid React component
 }
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Ensure the component returns a valid JSX element

// Wrong
const MyComponent = () => 42;

// Correct — return JSX, null, or a valid element
const MyComponent = () => <div>Hello</div>;
const MyComponent = () => null; // Valid in React

Fix 2: Install React type definitions

npm install --save-dev @types/react

Fix 3: Extend React.Component for class components

// Wrong
class MyComponent {
 render() {
 return <div>Hello</div>;
 }
}

// Correct
import React from "react";
class MyComponent extends React.Component {
 render() {
 return <div>Hello</div>;
 }
}

Fix 4: Use the correct function component signature

// Wrong — arrow function without proper typing
const MyComponent = (props) => <div>{props.name}</div>;

// Correct
interface Props {
 name: string;
}
const MyComponent: React.FC<Props> = ({ name }) => <div>{name}</div>;

Examples

// Example 1: Forwarded ref component
const MyComponent = React.forwardRef<HTMLDivElement>((props, ref) => {
 return <div ref={ref}>{props.children}</div>;
});
// If forwardRef types are wrong, TS2786 occurs

// Example 2: Higher-order component
function withAuth(Component: React.ComponentType) {
 return (props: any) => {
 if (!isLoggedIn()) return null;
 return <Component {...props} />; // TS2786 if ComponentType is wrong
 };
}

Related Errors

  • TS2762: JSX element missing closing tag — JSX syntax issues.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — prop type mismatches.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent props.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2794 — Expected 1 arguments but got 0
item.description:
TS2794 — Expected 1 arguments but got 0

TS2794 occurs when you call a function that requires arguments but provide none. In the context of incrementing, this often happens when trying to increment a value using a function that expects an argument.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Function requires an argument
function increment(value: number) {
 return value + 1;
}
increment(); // TS2794: Expected 1 arguments, but got 0

// Cause 2: Calling method without required parameter
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.indexOf(); // TS2794: Expected 1 arguments, but got 0

// Cause 3: Constructor expecting arguments
class Counter {
 constructor(initial: number) {}
}
new Counter(); // TS2794

How to Fix

Fix 1: Provide the required argument

increment(5);

Fix 2: Use optional parameters

function increment(value: number = 0) {
 return value + 1;
}
increment(); // OK — uses default

Fix 3: Use default parameter in constructor

class Counter {
 constructor(initial: number = 0) {}
}
new Counter(); // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — general argument count mismatch.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type issues.
  • TS2488: Type must have Symbol.iterator method — iterator errors.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2820 — Action Is Not a Recognized Action
item.description:
TS2820 — Action ‘X’ is not a recognized action

TS2820 occurs when you use an unrecognized action property in a RequestInit or similar fetch-related configuration. The action property expects specific HTTP method strings, and TypeScript validates that the value is one of the recognized methods.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in HTTP method
fetch("/api", { action: "get" }); // TS2820: 'get' is not a recognized action

// Cause 2: Using lowercase instead of uppercase
fetch("/api", { method: "get" }); // TS2820 if type expects uppercase

// Cause 3: Custom action string not in the union
fetch("/api", { action: "CUSTOM_ACTION" }); // TS2820

// Cause 4: Wrong property name
fetch("/api", { action: "GET" }); // TS2820 if 'action' isn't a valid property

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct HTTP method

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { action: "get" });

// Correct — use 'method' instead of 'action'
fetch("/api", { method: "GET" });

Fix 2: Use valid HTTP methods

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { method: "get" }); // lowercase

// Correct — uppercase
fetch("/api", { method: "GET" });

Fix 3: Use the correct RequestInit property

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { action: "POST" });

// Correct
fetch("/api", { method: "POST" });

Fix 4: Type the request options properly

const options: RequestInit = {
 method: "GET",
 headers: {
 "Content-Type": "application/json",
 },
};
fetch("/api", options);

Examples

// Example 1: Confusing 'action' with 'method'
fetch("/api/users", {
 action: "POST", // TS2820
 body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Alice" }),
});

// Fix:
fetch("/api/users", {
 method: "POST",
 body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Alice" }),
});

// Example 2: GraphQL fetch with custom options
fetch("/graphql", {
 method: "POST", // Not 'action'
 headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
 body: JSON.stringify({ query: "{ user { name } }" }),
});

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — using wrong property name.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — wrong type in function args.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS6133 — Variable 'X' is declared but never used
item.description:
TS6133 — Variable ‘X’ is declared but never used

TS6133 is a diagnostic warning (not a compile error) that occurs when a declared variable is never referenced. It is enabled by noUnusedLocals or noUnusedParameters.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Unused function parameter
function greet(name: string, age: number) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`; // TS6133: 'age' is declared but never used
}

// Cause 2: Unused destructured variable
const [first, second] = [1, 2]; // TS6133: 'second' is never used

// Cause 3: Unused import
import { readFile, writeFile } from "fs"; // TS6133: 'readFile' is never used

How to Fix

Fix 1: Remove the unused parameter

function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}

Fix 2: Prefix with underscore

function greet(name: string, _age: number) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}

Fix 3: Use the variable

const [first, second] = [1, 2];
console.log(first, second);

Fix 4: Disable the check

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "noUnusedParameters": false
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2309: Variable is declared but never read — local variable variant.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload issues.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7006 — Parameter 'X' implicitly has 'any' type
item.description:
TS7006 — Parameter ‘X’ implicitly has ‘any’ type

TS7006 occurs when noImplicitAny is enabled and TypeScript cannot infer a parameter’s type, defaulting it to any. This is a code quality flag — explicit types make code safer and more maintainable.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Callback without type annotation
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2); // TS7006 if noImplicitAny is on

// Cause 2: Event handler without type
element.addEventListener("click", event => {
 console.log(event.target); // TS7006
});

// Cause 3: Unannotated function parameter
function add(a, b) {
 return a + b; // TS7006 for both a and b
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add explicit type annotations

[1, 2, 3].map((x: number) => x * 2);

element.addEventListener("click", (event: MouseEvent) => {
 console.log(event.target);
});

function add(a: number, b: number): number {
 return a + b;
}

Fix 2: Use type inference from context

// TypeScript infers 'e' from addEventListener
element.addEventListener("click", (e) => {
 console.log(e.clientX); // OK — 'e' is inferred as MouseEvent
});

Fix 3: Disable noImplicitAny (not recommended)

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "noImplicitAny": false
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2683: Implicitly has type ‘any’ — this context implicit any.
  • TS18046: Variable is of type ‘unknown’ — unknown type usage.
  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructuring variant.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7006 — Parameter Implicitly Has 'any' Type
item.description:
TS7006 — Parameter ‘X’ implicitly has an ‘any’ type

TS7006 occurs when noImplicitAny is enabled (part of strict mode) and a function parameter has no type annotation and TypeScript can’t infer its type. This forces you to explicitly declare types for all parameters.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Untyped function parameter
function add(a, b) { // TS7006: Parameter 'a' implicitly has an 'any' type
 return a + b;
}

// Cause 2: Callback without type annotations
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2); // TS7006 if strict mode is on

// Cause 3: Event handler without type
document.addEventListener("click", (event) => { // TS7006: event implicitly has 'any'
 console.log(event.target);
});

// Cause 4: Destructured parameters without types
function process({ name, age }) { // TS7006
 console.log(name, age);
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add explicit type annotations

// Wrong
function add(a, b) {
 return a + b;
}

// Correct
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
 return a + b;
}

Fix 2: Type callback parameters

// Wrong
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2);

// Correct — TypeScript can usually infer this from the array type
// But if needed:
[1, 2, 3].map((x: number) => x * 2);

Fix 3: Use typed DOM event handlers

// Wrong
document.addEventListener("click", (event) => {});

// Correct
document.addEventListener("click", (event: MouseEvent) => {});

Fix 4: Type destructured parameters

// Wrong
function process({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
interface UserData {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
function process({ name, age }: UserData) {}

Examples

// Example 1: Arrow function in array method
const doubled = [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2); // TS7006: 'n' implicitly has 'any'

// Example 2: Event listener
button.addEventListener("click", e => {
 e.target; // TS7006
});

// Fix: button.addEventListener("click", (e: MouseEvent) => { ... });

Related Errors

  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructured parameters.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — type not imported.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — strict typing with unknown.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7006 v2 — Parameter Implicitly Has Any Type Fix
item.description:
TS7006 — Parameter Implicitly Has Any Type (v2)

This variant covers TS7006 errors in callback chains, event handlers, higher-order functions, and destructured parameters where the implicit any is harder to infer or annotate.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS7006: Parameter 'e' implicitly has an 'any' type
  • TS7006: Parameter 'err' implicitly has an 'any' type
  • TS7006: Parameter 'item' implicitly has an 'any' type
  • TS7006: Parameter 'data' implicitly has an 'any' type

When noImplicitAny is enabled (recommended), TypeScript requires all parameters to have explicit types. This catches many runtime errors at compile time.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7031 — Binding Element Implicitly Has 'any' Type
item.description:
TS7031 — Binding element ‘X’ implicitly has an ‘any’ type

TS7031 occurs when destructured parameters in a function don’t have type annotations and noImplicitAny is enabled. This is similar to TS7006 but specifically targets destructured binding elements.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Destructured function parameter without types
function greet({ name, age }) { // TS7031: 'name' implicitly has 'any' type
 return `Hello ${name}, you are ${age}`;
}

// Cause 2: Destructured in arrow functions
const process = ({ id, label }) => { // TS7031
 console.log(id, label);
};

// Cause 3: Destructured in method parameters
class Service {
 handle({ data, status }) { // TS7031
 return data;
 }
}

// Cause 4: Destructured with defaults but no type
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) { // TS7031
 console.log(host, port);
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Annotate the destructured parameter with an interface

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: User) {}

Fix 2: Use inline type annotations

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: { name: string; age: number }) {}

Fix 3: Type destructured defaults

// Wrong
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) {}

// Correct
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }: { host?: string; port?: number }) {}

Fix 4: Type arrow function parameters

// Wrong
const process = ({ id, label }) => {};

// Correct
const process = ({ id, label }: { id: number; label: string }) => {};

Examples

// Example 1: React component props
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }) { // TS7031
 return <button onClick={onClick} disabled={disabled}>{label}</button>;
}

// Fix: type the props
interface ButtonProps {
 onClick: () => void;
 label: string;
 disabled?: boolean;
}
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }: ButtonProps) {}

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — non-destructured parameters.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — related to object shape.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript tsconfig Error — Configuration Compilation Error
item.description:
TSConfig Error — TypeScript Configuration Compilation Error

A tsconfig error occurs when tsconfig.json contains invalid configuration, missing files, or incompatible options that prevent TypeScript from compiling your project.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Invalid JSON syntax
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "target": "es2020", // trailing comma is invalid in strict JSON
 }
}

// Cause 2: Referencing a non-existent file
{
 "files": ["src/main.ts", "src/missing.ts"]
}

// Cause 3: Conflicting options
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "module": "commonjs",
 "moduleResolution": "bundler"
 }
}

// Cause 4: Missing tsconfig.json when running tsc directly
// Running `tsc` without a config file or file arguments

How to Fix

Fix 1: Validate JSON syntax

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "target": "es2020",
 "module": "commonjs",
 "strict": true
 }
}

Fix 2: Use extends for shared configuration

{
 "extends": "@tsconfig/node18/tsconfig.json",
 "compilerOptions": {
 "outDir": "./dist",
 "rootDir": "./src"
 },
 "include": ["src/**/*"]
}

Fix 3: Verify file paths exist

# List files referenced in tsconfig
npx tsc --showConfig

Fix 4: Reset to a known-good configuration

npx tsc --init

Examples

# Show all effective configuration
npx tsc --showConfig

# Validate tsconfig without compiling
npx tsc --noEmit --project tsconfig.json

# List all files TypeScript would compile
npx tsc --listFiles

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — syntax error in config or source.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module resolution issues from bad config.

item.title: [Solution] VBA: Compile error: syntax error
item.description:
What This Error Means

VBA compile errors occur when the code violates VBA syntax rules. The VBA compiler detects these before the code runs, preventing execution until fixed.

Common Causes

  • Missing keywords (Sub, End Sub, If, Then)
  • Incorrect punctuation (missing colons, parentheses)
  • Unclosed strings or comments
  • Incorrect operator usage
  • Missing line continuation character

How to Fix

' WRONG: Missing End Sub
Sub Example1()
 MsgBox "Hello"
' Missing: End Sub

' CORRECT:
Sub Example1()
 MsgBox "Hello"
End Sub

' WRONG: Syntax error in If statement
Sub Example2()
 If x = 5 Then MsgBox "Five" ' Missing Then on new line
 Else MsgBox "Not five"
End If
End Sub

' CORRECT: Proper If syntax
Sub Example2()
 If x = 5 Then
 MsgBox "Five"
 Else
 MsgBox "Not five"
 End If
End Sub

' WRONG: Missing line continuation
Sub Example3()
 Dim result As String
 result = "This is a very long string that " &
 "needs to continue on the next line"
End Sub

' CORRECT: Use underscore for line continuation
Sub Example3()
 Dim result As String
 result = "This is a very long string that " & _
 "needs to continue on the next line"
End Sub

' CORRECT: Common syntax patterns
Sub Example4()
 ' For loop
 Dim i As Integer
 For i = 1 To 10
 Debug.Print i
 Next i
 
 ' Do loop
 Do While x < 10
 x = x + 1
 Loop
 
 ' Select Case
 Select Case x
 Case 1
 Debug.Print "One"
 Case Else
 Debug.Print "Other"
 End Select
End Sub

Related Errors

  • Compile Error: Variable - variable declaration
  • Compile Error: Argument - function calls
  • Runtime Error - execution errors

item.title: [Solution] VBA: Compile error: variable not defined
item.description:
What This Error Means

This compile error occurs when Option Explicit is used (which is recommended) and a variable is used without being declared first with Dim, Private, Public, or Static.

Common Causes

  • Option Explicit at top of module
  • Variable name typo
  • Missing Dim statement
  • Variable declared in wrong scope
  • Using undeclared variable name

How to Fix

' WRONG: Variable not declared
Option Explicit

Sub Example1()
 x = 5 ' Error: x not declared
 MsgBox x
End Sub

' CORRECT: Declare variable
Option Explicit

Sub Example1()
 Dim x As Integer
 x = 5
 MsgBox x
End Sub

' WRONG: Typo in variable name
Option Explicit

Sub Example2()
 Dim userName As String
 userName = "John"
 Debug.Print userNane ' Error: typo
End Sub

' CORRECT: Match variable name exactly
Option Explicit

Sub Example2()
 Dim userName As String
 userName = "John"
 Debug.Print userName
End Sub

' CORRECT: Declare all variables at top
Option Explicit

Sub Example3()
 Dim ws As Worksheet
 Dim rng As Range
 Dim i As Long
 Dim result As Double
 
 Set ws = ActiveSheet
 Set rng = ws.Range("A1:A10")
 
 For i = 1 To 10
 result = result + rng.Cells(i, 1).Value
 Next i
End Sub

' CORRECT: Module-level declarations
Option Explicit

Private mCounter As Long
Private mUserName As String

Public Sub UpdateCounter()
 mCounter = mCounter + 1
End Sub

Related Errors

  • Compile Error: Syntax - syntax issues
  • Compile Error: Argument - function issues
  • Type Mismatch - type errors

item.title: [Solution] VBA: Compile error: wrong number of arguments
item.description:
What This Error Means

This compile error occurs when you call a Sub or Function with a different number of arguments than it expects, or when required arguments are missing.

Common Causes

  • Missing required arguments
  • Extra arguments passed
  • Optional argument not provided when required
  • Function signature changed
  • Wrong argument order

How to Fix

' WRONG: Missing required argument
Sub Example1()
 MsgBox ' Error: MsgBox requires prompt argument
End Sub

' CORRECT: Provide required arguments
Sub Example1()
 MsgBox "Hello World"
End Sub

' WRONG: Extra arguments
Sub Example2()
 Dim result As Integer
 result = Abs(-5, 10) ' Error: Abs takes only 1 argument
End Sub

' CORRECT: Correct number of arguments
Sub Example2()
 Dim result As Integer
 result = Abs(-5) ' OK: 1 argument
End Sub

' CORRECT: Handle optional arguments
Function SafeProcess(data As Variant, Optional output As String = "") As Variant
 If output = "" Then
 SafeProcess = data
 Else
 SafeProcess = output & ": " & CStr(data)
 End If
End Function

Sub Example3()
 ' Call with optional argument
 Debug.Print SafeProcess(42) ' OK
 Debug.Print SafeProcess(42, "Result") ' OK
End Sub

' CORRECT: Check function signature
Sub Example4()
 ' Use Object Browser (F2) to check:
 ' - Number of arguments
 ' - Required vs Optional
 ' - Argument types
 
 ' Example: WorksheetFunction.VLookup
 Dim result As Variant
 result = Application.WorksheetFunction.VLookup( _
 "Key", Range("A1:B10"), 2, False)
End Sub

Related Errors

  • Compile Error: Syntax - syntax issues
  • Compile Error: Variable - declaration
  • Type Mismatch - argument types
